But that’s about it. The non-determinism is theoretical solvable. Chaos (prompt instability) isn’t. When you change a single word in the input, you get a completely different output. Because of this LLMs will never be compilers. Let they who has never ='d when they meant to == cast the first argument.(There's a reason why we benefit from code review). - its like going at the speed of light to achieve 100% accuracy.- you ll keep getting closer and closer but you ll never be at 100% as long as you have a non zero mass Sure, but it's at the point now where it can autocomplete a fragment of a sketch of an idea into a complete working product. No, it isn't. It can autocomplete a fragment of a sketch of an idea into something that may or may not match the original vision and almost certainly has serious bugs which only manifest over time because they elude a cursory test. Because Claude isn't a human the perception of it is different.Humans can fly planes, computers too can fly planes. I'm not going to give the same leeway to a 'computer' flying a plane that I would to a human. Because we understand that humans are just that, humans. The demands placed on a computer are so very different. What about driving a car?Computers are already driving cars autonomously, possibly better than the average human already, and quite plausibly will eventually be better than the best human driver. Precisely my point. Say for examples planes, trains or automobiles, humans may make way more errors than a computer does but you can bet your bottom dollar the threshold for allowing computers to autonomously control the above is way higher. Because the expectation is that if it is a computer it ought do it far better/safer. There is a lot of rehashing of LLM arguments in the comments here, but I'd love to read an actual distributed systems expert's take on the DNS architecture the author and/or Claude ended up with. One of my personal fears about LLM coding is that some of those implicitly outsourced decisions turn out to matter more than expected. Does the "timeline" stamp make sense? (I know there's not much detail to work from.) > exe.dev VMs have nice domain names: vm-name.exe.xyz. When we start a new VM, we add a CNAME entry or three. Easy, right?> But our VMs start fast, so fast that even if we created the DNS entries before creating the VM, our users still had to sit around waiting for DNS to propagate, which occasionally took minutes, not seconds.> We did the obvious thing: We wrote our own DNS server, so that DNS always immediately matched the source of truth. And life was good.> But latency matters, so we added regions. And just like that, DNS became the long pole again, because all DNS was served out of Oregon. Also, deployments caused tiny DNS outages. To fix this, all we needed now was a geographically distributed but fully consistent DNS server.> We did what a sensible engineer does when faced with a hard problem: cheat. We vibe-engineered a distributed DNS server tuned to our specific needs.I'm sure they have already considered this, but what problem does this solve that couldn't be solved by using dnsmasq, unbound or something like that? Why reinvent the wheel? The question is more what this really solves. They said they built all this because DNS propagation was too slow. But secondary dns servers by e.g. your isp will still have old records when you change something. So what did they earn? Every is correct within their network pretty fast. But for a normal user there isnt any difference because 8.8.8.8 or 1.1.1.1 still cache old records? This seems to be grasping for analogies to make sense of the work this person was doing.A compiler does a lot more than source code translation.There are specifications that tell us the de jure specifications of the language, if there is one, and then we have to recognize the de facto implementations of said language. The often disagree and leave much on the table. Some times on purpose, such as implementation details, and other times by omission.Users of this compiler expect a deterministic compilation of the source text into the target code but it’s rarely 1:1. There are optimization passes, inlining, barriers, etc.And then there are the run-time effects of executing a program!I think the analogy gets a little weak because natural language is not a precise enough language to specify discrete systems.What it sounds like the author is doing is bypassing decision points with other people and delaying making decisions themselves until the LLM agent forces them to? Which is a fine approach but I don’t think the analogy with a compiler is necessary.People seem to have a hard enough time understanding branch prediction and thread barriers. I still think it is (in the source-code generating mode) a compiler, just like LiquidHaskell is. (I haven't used LH but it essentially can automatically supply functions based on the type conditions you specify.)In my view, reasoning LLMs have learned a large number of close to true sentences of "logic" in natural language. These rules are not consistent (unlike typing rules of LH that allow creation of provably correct programs), but they work in practice better than LH. Essentially, they represent an unsound formal specification of natural language, together with many useful (almost)tautologies that helps solving constraint problems (just like LH does, but again unlike LLM, provably correctly).LLMs proved that human language can be formalized reasonably close to soundness. I believe we can have a sound formalization of human language, and that kind of formal language will be once superior to LLMs.It will also not be as complicated as LLMs and require only fraction of compute to use. The entire "Don't Read the Code" argument is just insane to me and ironically when you see who the people are that are advocating it, it's often the same people that talked about microservices, the latest java script framework, no-code or any other methodology that was in vogue at the time just to avoid focusing on solving the real problems; The illusion that they are just one "smart step" away from achieving a 10x productivity boost that will make everything else trivial... > By the time I was ready to build a keeper, I had accumulated a scar-tissue document that was empirically sufficient to guide an agent through most of the important decisions, at every layer, ranging from high level goals through architecture down to the occasional low level detail, such as the exact shape of the data type for load-bearing concurrent caches.Waterfall is dead, long live waterfall! I'll go a step further and say Claude is an Interpreter.It even has just-in-time compilation: it can directly follow a spec and generate small snippets live.I'm pretty sure we'll soon see services (admittedly highly inefficient) built on Claude-as-a-backend. Why inefficient? If Claude continuously makes the system more efficient based on demand, there's no reason for the system to be less efficient than if it was managed by humans. If Claude continuously makes the system more efficient based on demand Yes, Claude, please make the system as efficient as possible. Make no mistakes It's not. It's a cargo cult developer that produces more code than you can review and confidently pushes bugs to prod. Claude doesn't even make machine code though. Or it can if you really want to, but that's now how people use it. If you wanna include transpilers or interpreters, fine but the answer is still no, otherwise our git commits would just be prompts that get built either in CI or JIT. People deliver code, not prompts.Maybe that could change some day, but even then, it wouldn't be a useful designation. It's like saying "oh technically the Americas are an island." There's a reason we still distinguish between a transpiler and compiler. yo dawg! I heard you like distributed servicesSo I made a distributed service for your distributed name service so you can distribute your names to the service that distributes them on the onternet I'm kind of getting to the point where if I know something is vibe coded, I just won't use it. It's not an anti-AI thing, it's just a quality thing. Pretty much every piece of vibe coded software I've used has been bad in some regard. The worst ones are the ones that aren't obviously bad but rather subtly bad in dangerous ways (the article about OpenCode yesterday definitely made me nope out of using that) > I’d say that, in all the ways that matter, I understand the code. Sure, if I had to hand-edit it now, there’d be a serious learning curve. But I won't have to. And more importantly, I can reason about the system, share perspectives with my colleagues, and guide agents on future work.If there's a serious learning curve to editing code then you don't understand the code. We used to call that 'on-boarding' when you brought a new engineer on the team as they got up to speed as to how the codebase worked. The title is a reference back to "Is Claude Code a Compiler?" by the same author, linked from the first sentence: https://commaok.xyz/ai/is-claude-a-compiler/And then claim is attributed right there:> The hands-down highlight was the talk by Erik Schluntz: Vibe coding in prod.> Among other things, he drew an analogy between LLMs and compilers. Didn't read the article because the conclusion is obvoius enough to me as is, never heard of Erik Schluntz before, and drawing an analogy between two things means very little anyway. "this bike is about as heavy as that table" doesn't require the rebuttal that bikes aren't tables, for example.so far I mostly saw it spewed forth on HN Spec-driven development is sold on the idea that you regenerate modules or entire services when you change the spec. It's a really dumb idea but was briefly popular in enterprise, probably still is in some companies. A compiler does not transalte specs to code.Specs are denotational by nature.A program could synthesize a program that adheres to the specs. But that is not what we understand by a compiler that generally has to preserve operational semantics. I've heard the "if you hate vibe coding you must also hate compilers" strawman so many times, I'm sure it's been said by someone with a "name and reputation" at least once.

blocks.html_en:
LLM-is-a-compiler is indeed a simplistic approach. I wrote a rebuttal to the yesterday's Cursor post, may reuse it here https://replicated.live/blog/follow-up

The idea that a 835-page spec "just exists" and we run an LLM to implement it is completely flawed. Specs do not appear out of nowhere, they co-evolve with the code. If you have the code, why do you want to generate it again?

Good software is made as a product of numerous feedback loops and LLMs let you operate those loops faster. They do not supplement the entire process though.

In the end, a good product is a barrel of distilled feedback.

sentences.zh: 规范并不是凭空出现的，它们与代码共同演化。

sentences.zh: 但它们并不能补充整个过程。最终，好的产品是一桶经过提炼的反馈。

blocks.html_en:
Something I have always (even pre-LLMs) found funny to think about is - all code possible to run on a computer already exists. It's some permutation of all the bits of available memory. It's in there somewhere. So, suppose you want a specific program.

1. Some huge % of those possible programs are obviously not the one you want (most don't even compile).

2. Remaining programs might look similar to the one you want, but are buggy enough to be completely unusable, unreadable to the point of intractability, and so on.

3. Remaining programs look substantially correct but upon using for > a few mins you note major bugs that make it still not correct enough.

4. Remaining programs look substantially correct and seem to generally do your will but contain a long-tail showstopper subtle bug that corrupts saved data, or makes all the output subtly incorrect, etc.

5. Remaining programs might be useful, even if they're mildly annoying.

6. This process can probably continue for several thousand iterations until you finally find "it." The program you wanted. Or... one of them. There's probably still 10k+ candidate programs left at this stage.

Our job has always been to get to 5 and aspire to 6. Indeed most of software development is just doing 5->6 in a loop.

I think LLMs help us get to somewhere between 3 and 5 faster than we used to. And a big problem with them is that programs in 3 vs 5 all already look substantially correct and there's no way to know if you're getting 3 or 5.

Generally, the above is not how it _felt_ to write software pre-LLMs, it was just a cute way to imagine what you're doing. Now it's weirdly apropos.

sentences.zh: 我（甚至是法学硕士预科生）一直觉得有趣的事情是——所有可以在计算机上运行的代码都已经存在。

sentences.zh: 它就在那儿的某个地方。

sentences.zh: 因此，假设您想要一个特定的程序。1。

sentences.zh: 这些可能的程序中有很大一部分显然不是您想要的（大多数甚至无法编译）。2。

sentences.en: Remaining programs might look similar to the one you want, but are buggy enough to be completely unusable, unreadable to the point of intractability, and so on.3.
sentences.zh: 其余的程序可能看起来与您想要的程序类似，但有足够多的错误，以至于完全无法使用、无法读取到难以处理的程度，等等。3。

sentences.en: Remaining programs look substantially correct but upon using for > a few mins you note major bugs that make it still not correct enough.4.
sentences.zh: 其余程序看起来基本正确，但使用超过几分钟后，您会发现一些重大错误，使其仍然不够正确。4。

sentences.en: Remaining programs look substantially correct and seem to generally do your will but contain a long-tail showstopper subtle bug that corrupts saved data, or makes all the output subtly incorrect, etc.5.

sentences.en: Remaining programs might be useful, even if they're mildly annoying.6.

sentences.en: Remaining programs might look similar to the one you want, but are buggy enough to be completely unusable, unreadable to the point of intractability, and so on.
sentences.zh: 其余的程序可能看起来与您想要的程序类似，但有足够多的错误，以至于完全无法使用、无法读取到难以处理的程度，等等。

blocks.html_en: 3. Remaining programs look substantially correct but upon using for > a few mins you note major bugs that make it still not correct enough.

sentences.en: Remaining programs look substantially correct but upon using for > a few mins you note major bugs that make it still not correct enough.
sentences.zh: 其余程序看起来基本正确，但使用几分钟后，您会发现一些重大错误，使其仍然不够正确。

blocks.html_en: 4. Remaining programs look substantially correct and seem to generally do your will but contain a long-tail showstopper subtle bug that corrupts saved data, or makes all the output subtly incorrect, etc.

sentences.en: Remaining programs look substantially correct and seem to generally do your will but contain a long-tail showstopper subtle bug that corrupts saved data, or makes all the output subtly incorrect, etc.
sentences.zh: 其余的程序看起来基本上是正确的，并且似乎通常符合您的意愿，但包含一个长尾的令人惊叹的微妙错误，该错误会损坏保存的数据，或者使所有输出稍微不正确等。

blocks.html_en: 5. Remaining programs might be useful, even if they're mildly annoying.

sentences.en: Remaining programs might be useful, even if they're mildly annoying.
sentences.zh: 其余的程序可能有用，即使它们有点烦人。

blocks.html_en: 6. This process can probably continue for several thousand iterations until you finally find "it." The program you wanted. Or... one of them. There's probably still 10k+ candidate programs left at this stage.

sentences.en: This process can probably continue for several thousand iterations until you finally find "it."
sentences.zh: 这个过程可能会持续数千次迭代，直到您最终找到“它”。

sentences.en: The program you wanted.
sentences.zh: 你想要的程序。

sentences.en: Or...
sentences.zh: 或者...

sentences.en: one of them.
sentences.zh: 其中之一。

sentences.en: There's probably still 10k+ candidate programs left at this stage.
sentences.zh: 现阶段可能还剩下 10k+ 个候选程序。

blocks.html_en: Our job has always been to get to 5 and aspire to 6. Indeed most of software development is just doing 5->6 in a loop.

sentences.en: Our job has always been to get to 5 and aspire to 6.
sentences.zh: 我们的工作始终是达到 5 并渴望达到 6。

sentences.en: Indeed most of software development is just doing 5->6 in a loop.
sentences.zh: 事实上，大多数软件开发只是在循环中执行 5->6。

blocks.html_en: I think LLMs help us get to somewhere between 3 and 5 faster than we used to. And a big problem with them is that programs in 3 vs 5 all already look substantially correct and there's no way to know if you're getting 3 or 5.

sentences.en: I think LLMs help us get to somewhere between 3 and 5 faster than we used to.
sentences.zh: 我认为法学硕士可以帮助我们比以前更快地达到 3 到 5 倍。

sentences.en: And a big problem with them is that programs in 3 vs 5 all already look substantially correct and there's no way to know if you're getting 3 or 5.
sentences.zh: 它们的一个大问题是，3 vs 5 中的程序看起来都已经基本正确，并且无法知道您得到的是 3 还是 5。

blocks.html_en: Generally, the above is not how it _felt_ to write software pre-LLMs, it was just a cute way to imagine what you're doing. Now it's weirdly apropos.

sentences.en: Generally, the above is not how it _felt_ to write software pre-LLMs, it was just a cute way to imagine what you're doing.
sentences.zh: 一般来说，上面的内容并不是法学硕士预科阶段编写软件的感受，它只是一种想象你正在做什么的可爱方式。

sentences.en: Now it's weirdly apropos.
sentences.zh: 现在却很奇怪。

blocks.html_en: I once saw a YouTube video where two beginners were learning the basics programming, and upon opening a cmd prompt, one of them said "It's insane if you just type the right words into this box, you can steal someone's 401K in Arkansas" (Or something along those lines). It's always stuck with me that technically that's pretty correct. It's probably not how you'd go about it, but with the correct magic words in the right order it would work

sentences.en: I once saw a YouTube video where two beginners were learning the basics programming, and upon opening a cmd prompt, one of them said "It's insane if you just type the right words into this box, you can steal someone's 401K in Arkansas" (Or something along those lines).
sentences.zh: 我曾经看过一个 YouTube 视频，其中两个初学者正在学习基础编程，打开 cmd 提示符后，其中一个人说“如果你在这个框中输入正确的单词，你就可以窃取阿肯色州某人的 401K，这太疯狂了”（或者类似的内容）。

sentences.en: It's always stuck with me that technically that's pretty correct.
sentences.zh: 从技术上讲，这是相当正确的，这一点一直困扰着我。

sentences.en: It's probably not how you'd go about it, but with the correct magic words in the right order it would work
sentences.zh: 这可能不是你会做的，但是使用正确的魔法词以正确的顺序它会起作用

blocks.html_en:
One of my professors used an example like this to encourage you to pause if you ever find yourself doing something like "maybe if I just flip this < to > the tests will pass". The space of incorrect programs (even ones that pass your tests) is enormous compared to the space of correct programs.

If you like thinking about this and haven't seen this before, check out the busy beaver problem[1].

[1]: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Busy_beaver

sentences.zh: 我的一位教授用这样的例子来鼓励你在发现自己在做类似“也许如果我把这个 < 翻转到 > 测试就会通过”之类的事情时停下来。

sentences.zh: 与正确程序的空间相比，错误程序（即使是通过测试的程序）的空间是巨大的。如果您喜欢思考这个问题并且以前没有见过这个问题，请查看繁忙的海狸问题[1]。[1]：https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Busy_beaver

blocks.html_en:
all code possible to run on a computer already exists. It's some permutation of all the bits of available memory. It's in there somewhere

This seems either mathematically impossible or vacuous, depending on what you mean ...

sentences.zh: 它是所有可用内存位的某种排列。

sentences.zh: 它就在那儿的某个地方这在数学上似乎是不可能的或空洞的，具体取决于你的意思......

blocks.html_en:
I think the intent is clear. We could just enumerate all strings in size and lexicographical order. Then the task is only to filter, which strings are programs we desire.

It's the same sentiment as "the typing itself is not the issue, it's the order of the button presses that's difficult."

Of course, by Rice's theorem, all interesting questions are undecidable anyway...

sentences.en: I think the intent is clear.
sentences.zh: 我认为意图很明确。

sentences.en: We could just enumerate all strings in size and lexicographical order.
sentences.zh: 我们可以按照大小和字典顺序枚举所有字符串。

sentences.en: Then the task is only to filter, which strings are programs we desire.It's the same sentiment as "the typing itself is not the issue, it's the order of the button presses that's difficult."Of course, by Rice's theorem, all interesting questions are undecidable anyway...
sentences.zh: 那么任务只是过滤，哪些字符串是我们想要的程序。这与“打字本身不是问题，困难的是按钮按下的顺序”是一样的。当然，根据赖斯定理，所有有趣的问题无论如何都是不可判定的......

blocks.html_en: If we restrict ourselves to programs that can be stored inside the physical universe, suddenly everything switches from undecidable to O(1) :)

blocks.html_en:
It's the same mindset some artists have when considering raw stone for a statue: there's a Greek god in there somewhere, you just have to find him.

It's a way of thinking, not some deep metaphysical mystery. Unless it is actually a deep metaphysical mystery, but I'd rather not go there.

sentences.zh: 这与一些艺术家在考虑用原石制作雕像时的心态相同：那里有一个希腊神，你只需要找到他。这是一种思维方式，而不是一些深刻的形而上学之谜。

sentences.en: Unless it is actually a deep metaphysical mystery, but I'd rather not go there.
sentences.zh: 除非它实际上是一个深刻的形而上学之谜，但我宁愿不去那里。

blocks.html_en:
You should check out Borges’, The Library of Babel.

Basically if you have an infinite string of characters in some language then you have every possible book, tweet, script, scribble, and scrap of everything ever written. You just have to find it.

Thats already a large universe to cover. Programs? Much bigger set.

sentences.en: You should check out Borges’, The Library of Babel.Basically if you have an infinite string of characters in some language then you have every possible book, tweet, script, scribble, and scrap of everything ever written.
sentences.zh: 你应该看看博尔赫斯的《巴别塔图书馆》。基本上，如果你有某种语言的无限字符串，那么你就拥有所有可能的书籍、推文、脚本、潦草的文字和所有曾经写过的东西。

sentences.en: You just have to find it.Thats already a large universe to cover.
sentences.zh: 你只需要找到它。那已经是一个广阔的宇宙了。

sentences.en: Programs?
sentences.zh: 节目？

sentences.en: Much bigger set.
sentences.zh: 更大的集合。

blocks.html_en: I believe it's more that the library contains the finite set of all books which have a (unique) permutation of the language's characters to size N. The challenge to finding the cure to cancer in such a collection of sufficiently large N is indeed finding which book it is in.

sentences.en: I believe it's more that the library contains the finite set of all books which have a (unique) permutation of the language's characters to size N.
sentences.zh: 我相信更重要的是，图书馆包含所有书籍的有限集，这些书籍对语言字符进行（唯一）排列，大小为 N。

sentences.en: The challenge to finding the cure to cancer in such a collection of sufficiently large N is indeed finding which book it is in.
sentences.zh: 在如此大的 N 集合中找到癌症治疗方法的挑战实际上是找到它在哪本书中。

> all code possible to run on a computer already exists

You have to be careful; that line of thinking might get you trapped in L-space. You’d need a very skilled librarian and possibly a bag of peanuts to get back out.

blocks.html_en: There are plenty of processes that have this property, though. For example: setting up a home, workspace, kitchen, factory, office, etc. to fit a particular purpose. As you discover what works well in a particular space, you will refactor your workflows, seating organization, storage systems, etc to fit what you're doing. And it may even have multiple valid configurations - for example seasonal variations like outdoor vs indoor areas.

blocks.html_en:
> It's some permutation of all the bits of available memory. It's in there somewhere.

True, but the number of possible permutations is so large it's incomprehensible. Let's limit ourselves to say a 50 MiB program, meaning the size of the compiled binary. If I'm remembering the math correctly, that amount of memory can hold 2^(2^20 * 50 * 8) different states. That's 2^419430400 or roughly 10^126000000. For comparison, the heat death of the universe is predicted to occur in about 10^100 years, or 10^107 seconds. You'd need to be able to look at more than a million programs per second to examine them all before the end of the universe.

sentences.en: > It's some permutation of all the bits of available memory.
sentences.zh: > 这是所有可用内存位的某种排列。

sentences.en: It's in there somewhere.True, but the number of possible permutations is so large it's incomprehensible.
sentences.zh: 它就在那儿的某个地方。确实如此，但是可能的排列数量太大了，难以理解。

sentences.zh: 我们将程序限制为 50 MiB，即编译后的二进制文件的大小。

sentences.zh: 如果我没记错的话，那么内存量可以容纳 2^(2^20 * 50 * 8) 种不同的状态。

sentences.en: For comparison, the heat death of the universe is predicted to occur in about 10^100 years, or 10^107 seconds.
sentences.zh: 作为比较，宇宙的热寂预计将在大约 10^100 年或 10^107 秒内发生。

sentences.en: You'd need to be able to look at more than a million programs per second to examine them all before the end of the universe.
sentences.zh: 您需要能够每秒查看超过一百万个程序，才能在宇宙终结之前检查所有程序。

blocks.html_en: Reality is a fractal (Mandelbrot?) and this is where LLMs will help science. They will help us explore the fuzzy edges faster. LLMs probably won't find new "leaves", but they should be able to cover a lot of useful but "boring" fuzz.

sentences.en: Reality is a fractal (Mandelbrot?)
sentences.zh: 现实是分形的（曼德尔布罗特？）

sentences.en: and this is where LLMs will help science.
sentences.zh: 这就是法学硕士将帮助科学的地方。

sentences.en: They will help us explore the fuzzy edges faster.
sentences.zh: 它们将帮助我们更快地探索模糊边缘。

sentences.en: LLMs probably won't find new "leaves", but they should be able to cover a lot of useful but "boring" fuzz.
sentences.zh: 法学硕士可能不会找到新的“叶子”，但他们应该能够涵盖许多有用但“无聊”的模糊问题。

blocks.html_en:
In my experience, a spec is often public (or available for purchase by the public from a standards committee for a few hundred dollars, close enough in business terms), but the source code for your competitor's implementations is not available for any price. The first barrier to entry to become a competitor in many industries is to implement an 835 page (note that those are rookie numbers) spec, building software that is compatible with existing data formats or legal frameworks or network protocols or hardware or whatever.

This is another example of the many invisible silos that developers end up in:

1. Some are developing enterprise software for use by their own employer, the spec only exists because it's useful to help the dev team manage the project. In this case, it's totally reasonable to expect the spec to co-evolve with the code. If the release gets pushed out to Q3 or the target has to be adjusted due to unforseen implementation difficulties, so be it.

2. Some are developing contract software for their customers. The spec exists as a communication tool and requirements that allows the developers to know what to build and to inform the customer that what they've built has reached a certain phase of the billing process (including completion). You're on a clock, and if you're late or you don't do what you said you'd do then your customer isn't going to pay you.

3. Some are developing software for abstract customers - either B2B or B2C. Often, the specs here are regulatory or industry-standard, implementing the spec is the function of the business. You're releasing updates and new products to make year-end sales targets or snipe announcements by your competition, and advertising compatibility with various standards is a crucial bullet point in the sales presentation.

Bug reports, feature requests, and especially deadlines have different meanings in each context. The way AI development agents can be used in each silo also varies enormously!

blocks.html_en:
In my experience, a spec is often public (or available for purchase by the public from a standards committee for a few hundred dollars, close enough in business terms), but the source code for your competitor's implementations is not available for any price. The first barrier to entry to become a competitor in many industries is to implement an 835 page (note that those are rookie numbers) spec, building software that is compatible with existing data formats or legal frameworks or network protocols or hardware or whatever.

This is another example of the many invisible silos that developers end up in:

1. Some are developing enterprise software for use by their own employer, the spec only exists because it's useful to help the dev team manage the project. In this case, it's totally reasonable to expect the spec to co-evolve with the code. If the release gets pushed out to Q3 or the target has to be adjusted due to unforseen implementation difficulties, so be it.

2. Some are developing contract software for their customers. The spec exists as a communication tool and requirements that allows the developers to know what to build and to inform the customer that what they've built has reached a certain phase of the billing process (including completion). You're on a clock, and if you're late or you don't do what you said you'd do then your customer isn't going to pay you.

3. Some are developing software for abstract customers - either B2B or B2C. Often, the specs here are regulatory or industry-standard, implementing the spec is the function of the business. You're releasing updates and new products to make year-end sales targets or snipe announcements by your competition, and advertising compatibility with various standards is a crucial bullet point in the sales presentation.

Bug reports, feature requests, and especially deadlines have different meanings in each context. The way AI development agents can be used in each silo also varies enormously!

blocks.html_en:
As a counter argument for the compiler perspective: what Cursor did is basically transpiling a project to another language, which is a form of compilation.

If you distill the feedback into a spec (probably a combination of both docs and tests) which comprehensively covers the functional requirements, then you could treat the code as cached materialization. The value of generating code again is targeting different non-functional requirements, like ability to run in different environments or catering to different performance characteristics.

sentences.en: As a counter argument for the compiler perspective: what Cursor did is basically transpiling a project to another language, which is a form of compilation.If you distill the feedback into a spec (probably a combination of both docs and tests) which comprehensively covers the functional requirements, then you could treat the code as cached materialization.
sentences.zh: 作为编译器角度的反驳：Cursor 所做的基本上是将项目转换为另一种语言，这是编译的一种形式。如果您将反馈提炼成全面涵盖功能需求的规范（可能是文档和测试的组合），那么您可以将代码视为缓存的具体化。

sentences.en: The value of generating code again is targeting different non-functional requirements, like ability to run in different environments or catering to different performance characteristics.
sentences.zh: 再次生成代码的价值在于针对不同的非功能性需求，例如在不同环境中运行的能力或满足不同的性能特征。

blocks.html_en:
I agree with iteration, but that's an orthogonal argument to how effective it is at turning prose to code. You are asking, LLMs are capable of translating a 835-page spec, but where does the spec come from? Follow the stream up from the built software product:

Binary < IR < Code < Spec < Prompt (aka goals)

For LLMs to faithfully fulfill the role of translating spec/prompt to code, it needs enough information about the problem/motivation and the constraints around it. Often, that's done by research or by importing proven modules. The human role left in this compilation chain is a precise-enough expression of the motivation, upstream of the built artifacts.

Edit For example, SWEbench Pro was supposed to be a measure for how good LLMs are at compiling prose into code. At least 15% of those tasks were underdetermined by ambiguous prose in the spec. Of the issues that are specific enough, Claude and Codex indeed do compile to functional code with self-written tests. Source: https://github.com/kimjune01/swebench-pro-audit

sentences.en: I agree with iteration, but that's an orthogonal argument to how effective it is at turning prose to code.
sentences.zh: 我同意迭代，但这是一个关于将散文转化为代码的有效性的正交论证。

sentences.en: You are asking, LLMs are capable of translating a 835-page spec, but where does the spec come from?
sentences.zh: 您可能会问，LLM 能够翻译 835 页的规范，但规范从何而来？

sentences.en: Often, that's done by research or by importing proven modules.
sentences.zh: 通常，这是通过研究或导入经过验证的模块来完成的。

sentences.en: At least 15% of those tasks were underdetermined by ambiguous prose in the spec.
sentences.zh: 这些任务中至少有 15% 是由于规范中含糊不清的文字而未能充分确定的。

blocks.html_en: I agree with iteration, but that's an orthogonal argument to how effective it is at turning prose to code. You are asking, LLMs are capable of translating a 835-page spec, but where does the spec come from? Follow the stream up from the built software product:

sentences.en: I agree with iteration, but that's an orthogonal argument to how effective it is at turning prose to code.
sentences.zh: 我同意迭代，但这是一个关于将散文转化为代码的有效性的正交论证。

blocks.html_en: Binary < IR < Code < Spec < Prompt (aka goals)

sentences.en: Binary < IR < Code < Spec < Prompt (aka goals)
sentences.zh: 二进制 < IR < 代码 < 规格 < 提示（又名目标）

blocks.html_en: For LLMs to faithfully fulfill the role of translating spec/prompt to code, it needs enough information about the problem/motivation and the constraints around it. Often, that's done by research or by importing proven modules. The human role left in this compilation chain is a precise-enough expression of the motivation, upstream of the built artifacts.

sentences.en: For LLMs to faithfully fulfill the role of translating spec/prompt to code, it needs enough information about the problem/motivation and the constraints around it.
sentences.zh: 为了让法学硕士忠实地履行将规范/提示翻译为代码的角色，需要有关问题/动机及其周围约束的足够信息。

sentences.en: Often, that's done by research or by importing proven modules.
sentences.zh: 通常，这是通过研究或导入经过验证的模块来完成的。

sentences.en: The human role left in this compilation chain is a precise-enough expression of the motivation, upstream of the built artifacts.
sentences.zh: 留在这个编译链中的人类角色是对构建工件上游动机的足够精确的表达。

blocks.html_en:
Indeed, this is a much more realistic and useful framing of what to do with the current capabilities of LLMs. They work so fast, at such a high level, that you can speedrun the co-evolution process and end up with a spec and code that works. You can actually use second-system (fourth? fifth? I lost track) syndrome to your advantage because it’s so cheap to throw things away. And they don’t just write the system, they also write the test harness, the benchmarks, the failure simulation, the statistics…all the stuff that seems like “overhead” but lets you drive that evolution with data.

I’ve kind of drifted into this mode a bit at a time over the last year, but hadn’t stepped back to make such a coherent explanation of it.

sentences.en: Indeed, this is a much more realistic and useful framing of what to do with the current capabilities of LLMs.
sentences.zh: 事实上，对于如何利用法学硕士的当前能力，这是一个更加现实和有用的框架。

sentences.en: They work so fast, at such a high level, that you can speedrun the co-evolution process and end up with a spec and code that works.
sentences.zh: 它们的工作速度如此之快，水平如此之高，以至于您可以加速共同进化过程，并最终得到有效的规范和代码。

sentences.zh: 您实际上可以使用第二系统（第四？

sentences.en: fifth?
sentences.zh: 第五？

sentences.zh: 我忘记了）综合症对你有利，因为扔掉东西太便宜了。

blocks.html_en: The source code always was the result of that distillation, or at least a crucial part of the result. I’m unconvinced that natural-language specs can replace that role. Even long before LLMs, when writing specs I often resort to some form of formal language to precisely nail down certain parts of the specification. And some parts are always left open, where I know I’ll get to them when writing the code, because they are more effectively solved “in the code” than waterfall-like up front.

sentences.en: The source code always was the result of that distillation, or at least a crucial part of the result.
sentences.zh: 源代码始终是蒸馏的结果，或者至少是结果的关键部分。

sentences.en: I’m unconvinced that natural-language specs can replace that role.
sentences.zh: 我不相信自然语言规范可以取代这个角色。

sentences.en: Even long before LLMs, when writing specs I often resort to some form of formal language to precisely nail down certain parts of the specification.
sentences.zh: 甚至早在法学硕士之前，在编写规范时，我就经常采用某种形式的形式语言来精确确定规范的某些部分。

sentences.en: And some parts are always left open, where I know I’ll get to them when writing the code, because they are more effectively solved “in the code” than waterfall-like up front.
sentences.zh: 有些部分总是保持开放状态，我知道在编写代码时我会接触到它们，因为它们在“代码中”比预先瀑布式地解决更有效。

blocks.html_en:
A zen master asked a junior engineer to go get him a rock. The junior engineer asked what kind of rock he should get and the master replied by knocking him in the head with his cane. The junior thought for a minute, then asked, "would you like a round rock or a flat rock?" Again, the zen master rapped him on the head. The junior thought for a moment, then went to the creek and fetched a rock at random. He brought it to the zen master who inspected it and then said, "No, I want a flat piece of flint for starting a fire." The junior engineer was then enlightened.

Stakeholders will only give you requirements after they see the prototype. Thus, the spec and the prototype to elicit it are two sides to the same coin. Once the spec if correct and the prototype is starting to show its age, throw away the prototype and implement the spec with a fresh start.

sentences.en: A zen master asked a junior engineer to go get him a rock.
sentences.zh: 一位禅宗大师让一位初级工程师去给他找一块石头。

sentences.en: The junior engineer asked what kind of rock he should get and the master replied by knocking him in the head with his cane.
sentences.zh: 初级工程师问他应该得到什么样的岩石，大师用拐杖敲了敲他的头来回答。

sentences.en: The junior thought for a minute, then asked, "would you like a round rock or a flat rock?"
sentences.zh: 小辈想了想，问道：“你想要圆石还是扁石？”

sentences.en: Again, the zen master rapped him on the head.
sentences.zh: 禅师再次敲打他的头。

sentences.en: The junior thought for a moment, then went to the creek and fetched a rock at random.
sentences.zh: 小辈想了想，就到小溪边随意捡了一块石头。

sentences.en: He brought it to the zen master who inspected it and then said, "No, I want a flat piece of flint for starting a fire."
sentences.zh: 他把它拿给禅师检查，然后说：“不，我想要一块扁平的打火石来生火。”

sentences.en: The junior engineer was then enlightened.Stakeholders will only give you requirements after they see the prototype.
sentences.zh: 初级工程师这才恍然大悟，利益相关者只有看到原型后才会给你需求。

sentences.en: Thus, the spec and the prototype to elicit it are two sides to the same coin.
sentences.zh: 因此，规范和引发它的原型是同一枚硬币的两个方面。

sentences.en: Once the spec if correct and the prototype is starting to show its age, throw away the prototype and implement the spec with a fresh start.
sentences.zh: 一旦规范正确并且原型开始显示出它的年龄，就扔掉原型并重新开始实施规范。

blocks.html_en:
> Stakeholders will only give you requirements after they see the prototype.

That’s very much incorrect and sounds like someone who love building more than communicating. Even without a prototype, users and other consumers will be able to explain their problems. It may not use the same metaphor map that you’re used to. But it’s very much a description of the problem space.

Once you design a prototype, what you will get is feedback about the solution you’ve designed, not the raw problem. If you’ve not listened well in the first place you may be well off the mark, and have to work harder to correct things.

Even if your story, the junior would do way less work by asking the master what he intends to do with the piece of work instead of focusing on the rock itself.

sentences.en: > Stakeholders will only give you requirements after they see the prototype.That’s very much incorrect and sounds like someone who love building more than communicating.
sentences.zh: > 利益相关者只有在看到原型后才会向您提出要求。这是非常不正确的，听起来像是一个更喜欢构建而不是沟通的人。

sentences.en: Even without a prototype, users and other consumers will be able to explain their problems.
sentences.zh: 即使没有原型，用户和其他消费者也能够解释他们的问题。

sentences.en: It may not use the same metaphor map that you’re used to.
sentences.zh: 它可能不会使用您习惯的隐喻图。

sentences.en: But it’s very much a description of the problem space.Once you design a prototype, what you will get is feedback about the solution you’ve designed, not the raw problem.
sentences.zh: 但这在很大程度上是对问题空间的描述。一旦你设计了原型，你将得到的是关于你设计的解决方案的反馈，而不是原始问题。

sentences.en: If you’ve not listened well in the first place you may be well off the mark, and have to work harder to correct things.Even if your story, the junior would do way less work by asking the master what he intends to do with the piece of work instead of focusing on the rock itself.
sentences.zh: 如果你一开始就没有听清楚，你可能会偏离主题，并且必须更加努力地纠正问题。即使你的故事，初级者也会通过询问大师他打算如何处理这件作品而不是专注于岩石本身来减少工作量。

blocks.html_en:
> Stakeholders will only give you requirements after they see the prototype.

That’s very much incorrect and sounds like someone who love building more than communicating. Even without a prototype, users and other consumers will be able to explain their problems. It may not use the same metaphor map that you’re used to. But it’s very much a description of the problem space.

Once you design a prototype, what you will get is feedback about the solution you’ve designed, not the raw problem. If you’ve not listened well in the first place you may be well off the mark, and have to work harder to correct things.

Even if your story, the junior would do way less work by asking the master what he intends to do with the piece of work instead of focusing on the rock itself.

blocks.html_en: The cost of prototyping has dropped so much due to LLMs and coding agents that it basically always makes sense to prototype something to elicit feedback, just as part of the requirements gathering process. E.g. if you can spend $50 to understand something that will save you two weeks of effort, wouldn't you do that? That's the sort of dynamic I'm talking about.

sentences.zh: 如果你能花 50 美元去了解一些可以节省你两周精力的东西，你会不会这么做？

sentences.en: That's the sort of dynamic I'm talking about.
sentences.zh: 这就是我所说的那种动态。

blocks.html_en:
The cost of prototyping has always been very low. I can quickly sketch a UX flow using balsamiq. Or copy-paste paste code from docs and examples to show a rough working model with. If you can extract the essence of the problem you can quickly realize cheap ways to demonstrate a solution, and not spend two weeks trying to code it.

If there’s something that is wrong with using LLMs to prototype, it is that those prototypes are always too complex. Instead of decomposing a problem and testing unrelated concerns apart from each other, it’s often a mix of everything that the LLM user had been thinking about.

Often there’s no rapid iteration where you are comparing several approaches. Instead it became tunnel vision and confirmation bias where if it’s working, that means it’s the solution.

sentences.en: The cost of prototyping has always been very low.
sentences.zh: 原型制作的成本一直非常低。

sentences.en: I can quickly sketch a UX flow using balsamiq.
sentences.zh: 我可以使用 balsamiq 快速绘制用户体验流程。

sentences.en: Or copy-paste paste code from docs and examples to show a rough working model with.
sentences.zh: 或者从文档和示例中复制粘贴代码以显示粗略的工作模型。

sentences.en: If you can extract the essence of the problem you can quickly realize cheap ways to demonstrate a solution, and not spend two weeks trying to code it.If there’s something that is wrong with using LLMs to prototype, it is that those prototypes are always too complex.
sentences.zh: 如果你能提取问题的本质，你就可以快速找到演示解决方案的廉价方法，而不必花两周时间尝试编码。如果说使用法学硕士进行原型有什么问题的话，那就是这些原型总是太复杂了。

sentences.en: Instead of decomposing a problem and testing unrelated concerns apart from each other, it’s often a mix of everything that the LLM user had been thinking about.Often there’s no rapid iteration where you are comparing several approaches.
sentences.zh: 它不是分解问题并单独测试不相关的问题，而是通常混合了 LLM 用户一直在考虑的所有内容。通常没有快速迭代来比较几种方法。

sentences.en: Instead it became tunnel vision and confirmation bias where if it’s working, that means it’s the solution.
sentences.zh: 相反，它变成了狭隘视野和确认偏见，如果它有效，那就意味着它就是解决方案。

blocks.html_en:
Well, since we started training them on RLHF-style rating of the last generation they've totally collapsed back onto predictable suffix generators, which is why there are desperate, low-AUC BERT-inspired clasufiers bolted to them now.

They have like, Chomsky grammars now. And you can't fix it, it's structural to the process. You have to rewind almost to the pre-train.

sentences.en: Well, since we started training them on RLHF-style rating of the last generation they've totally collapsed back onto predictable suffix generators, which is why there are desperate, low-AUC BERT-inspired clasufiers bolted to them now.They have like, Chomsky grammars now.
sentences.zh: 好吧，自从我们开始用上一代的 RLHF 风格评级来训练它们以来，它们已经完全崩溃回到可预测的后缀生成器上，这就是为什么现在有绝望的、低 AUC BERT 启发的分类器紧随其后。它们现在有乔姆斯基语法。

sentences.en: And you can't fix it, it's structural to the process.
sentences.zh: 而且你无法修复它，它是流程的结构性问题。

sentences.en: You have to rewind almost to the pre-train.
sentences.zh: 你必须快退到前车。

blocks.html_en:
The argument being made here is really incredible when you unpack it.

1) The construction of the Empire State Building was particularly effective due to the depth of human-to-human collaboration.

2) Isn't it great that we can burn a bunch of dinosaur blood to convince ourselves that we don't need other humans?

sentences.en: The argument being made here is really incredible when you unpack it.1) The construction of the Empire State Building was particularly effective due to the depth of human-to-human collaboration.2) Isn't it great that we can burn a bunch of dinosaur blood to convince ourselves that we don't need other humans?
sentences.zh: 当你打开包装时，这里提出的论点真的令人难以置信。1）由于人与人之间合作的深度，帝国大厦的建设特别有效。2）我们可以燃烧一堆恐龙的血来说服自己我们不需要其他人类，这不是很棒吗？

blocks.html_en:
This is a company selling picks and shovels for the AI bubble. Of course they're going to drip feed "AI is so awesome guys!" stories on their blog.

If you strip away the hyperbolic statement about compilers and the Empire State Building, it's an internal success anecdote.

Would be interested to hear how all of the self-directed AI decisions worked out after launch. This is where I run into problems with AI. Sometimes it makes product decisions that aren't immediately visible and make no sense and cause serious problems down the road. Also, an analysis of how much time was actually saved by using AI? since it sounds like there was a lot of guidance and testing to make sure what the AI did was correct.

blocks.html_en:
> 2) Isn't it great that we can burn a bunch of dinosaur blood to convince ourselves that we don't need other humans?

God I hate this cynical take. Yes human beings use energy. We should look to generate more energy as that's a sign of wealth. The "energy bad" take is so lazy and ultimately anti-human. And in this case its especially obnoxious because what does "we don't need other humans" even mean.

blocks.html_en:
Headline true, article false.

AI exists at the boundary of codification. When you understand something well enough to express it in a deterministic way, it is no longer worthwhile to keep asking AI for that thing over and over - you should codify it.

But wait, I hear you say? AI has knowledge! And we can rely on that knowledge and sprinkle on top some brief instructions and it'll get things right and work out the details itself!

That is spec driven development.

And this is great one single time. Except, knowledge shifts. You are outsourcing part of what you've codified to society as a whole, in the form of language model knowledge. You're also trusting current and future models to always produce something that meets the requirements, and never be quantized under you, and produce the same result next time despite being a probabilistic system with no memory, and and and.

Better to freeze everything in place in your codebase so you get predictable results - in other words, codify it! Let's store the desired state of that button in code in a very complicated thing that's existed for decades called a string, so it always says "Finish" and never says "Done" - even if we regenerate the app.

That's pretty unexciting and doesn't justify tens of billions of investment. Which means you won't hear it from anyone with tokens to sell. But it's what you have to do to make something people want.

sentences.en: Headline true, article false.AI exists at the boundary of codification.
sentences.zh: 标题正确，文章错误。人工智能存在于编纂的边界。

sentences.en: When you understand something well enough to express it in a deterministic way, it is no longer worthwhile to keep asking AI for that thing over and over - you should codify it.But wait, I hear you say?
sentences.zh: 当你对某件事的理解足够好，能够以确定性的方式表达它时，就不再值得一遍又一遍地向人工智能询问那件事了——你应该将其编入法典。但是等等，我听到你说？

sentences.en: AI has knowledge!
sentences.zh: 人工智能有知识！

sentences.en: And we can rely on that knowledge and sprinkle on top some brief instructions and it'll get things right and work out the details itself!That is spec driven development.And this is great one single time.
sentences.zh: 我们可以依靠这些知识并在上面撒上一些简短的说明，它就会把事情做好并自行解决细节！这就是规范驱动的开发。这一次就很棒了。

sentences.en: Except, knowledge shifts.
sentences.zh: 除了知识的转变。

sentences.en: You are outsourcing part of what you've codified to society as a whole, in the form of language model knowledge.
sentences.zh: 您正在以语言模型知识的形式将您编写的部分内容外包给整个社会。

sentences.en: You're also trusting current and future models to always produce something that meets the requirements, and never be quantized under you, and produce the same result next time despite being a probabilistic system with no memory, and and and.Better to freeze everything in place in your codebase so you get predictable results - in other words, codify it!
sentences.zh: 您还相信当前和未来的模型始终会产生满足要求的东西，并且永远不会在您的控制下进行量化，并且下次会产生相同的结果，尽管它是一个没有记忆的概率系统，并且和和。最好将所有内容冻结在代码库中，以便获得可预测的结果 - 换句话说，将其编码！

sentences.en: Let's store the desired state of that button in code in a very complicated thing that's existed for decades called a string, so it always says "Finish" and never says "Done" - even if we regenerate the app.That's pretty unexciting and doesn't justify tens of billions of investment.
sentences.zh: 让我们将该按钮的所需状态存储在代码中一个非常复杂的东西中，这个东西已经存在了几十年，称为字符串，因此它总是说“完成”，而永远不会说“完成”——即使我们重新生成应用程序。这相当平淡，也不能证明数百亿投资的合理性。

sentences.en: Which means you won't hear it from anyone with tokens to sell.
sentences.zh: 这意味着您不会从任何有代币出售的人那里听到这样的消息。

sentences.en: But it's what you have to do to make something people want.
sentences.zh: 但这是你必须做的事情才能做出人们想要的东西。

sentences.en: Let's store the desired state of that button in code in a very complicated thing that's existed for decades called a string, so it always says "Finish" and never says "Done" - even if we regenerate the app.
sentences.zh: 让我们将该按钮的所需状态存储在代码中一个非常复杂的东西中，该东西已经存在了几十年，称为字符串，因此它总是说“完成”，而从不说“完成” - 即使我们重新生成应用程序。

blocks.html_en: That's pretty unexciting and doesn't justify tens of billions of investment. Which means you won't hear it from anyone with tokens to sell. But it's what you have to do to make something people want.

sentences.en: That's pretty unexciting and doesn't justify tens of billions of investment.
sentences.zh: 这相当令人兴奋，也无法证明数百亿美元的投资是合理的。

sentences.en: Which means you won't hear it from anyone with tokens to sell.
sentences.zh: 这意味着您不会从任何有代币出售的人那里听到这样的消息。

sentences.en: But it's what you have to do to make something people want.
sentences.zh: 但这是你必须做的事情才能做出人们想要的东西。

blocks.html_en:
I've heard something along the lines of "Claude is like a compiler: source code is the new object code, you don't look at that anymore" many times.

And I don't really think this is true. Compilers are usually deterministic, and whilst we can find edge cases, it's nothing like an AI agent writing all the code for you.

I think you have two choices, given the Claude is a code generator and not a compiler: (a) you review most or all of code to make sure it makes sense, or (b) you trust but verify via a strong test suite, potentially also created by Claude.

The problem with (a) is that you lose a lot of the speed-up. The problem with (b) is that you have no human oversight and the code may be incomplete, badly designed, or plain wrong.

Currently we review all code because correctness is extremely important to what we do, but that comes at a cost.

I don't know what the answer is here, in general. Does trust build over time? Do the models just get so good we can trust them to make zero mistakes?

sentences.en: I've heard something along the lines of "Claude is like a compiler: source code is the new object code, you don't look at that anymore" many times.And I don't really think this is true.
sentences.zh: 我多次听到“克劳德就像一个编译器：源代码是新的目标代码，你不再看它了”之类的话。但我真的不认为这是真的。

sentences.en: Compilers are usually deterministic, and whilst we can find edge cases, it's nothing like an AI agent writing all the code for you.I think you have two choices, given the Claude is a code generator and not a compiler: (a) you review most or all of code to make sure it makes sense, or (b) you trust but verify via a strong test suite, potentially also created by Claude.The problem with (a) is that you lose a lot of the speed-up.
sentences.zh: 编译器通常是确定性的，虽然我们可以找到边缘情况，但这并不像人工智能代理为你编写所有代码。我认为你有两种选择，因为克劳德是一个代码生成器而不是编译器：（a）你审查大部分或全部代码以确保它有意义，或者（b）你信任但通过强大的测试套件进行验证，可能也是由克劳德创建的。（a）的问题是你失去了很多加速。

sentences.en: The problem with (b) is that you have no human oversight and the code may be incomplete, badly designed, or plain wrong.Currently we review all code because correctness is extremely important to what we do, but that comes at a cost.I don't know what the answer is here, in general.
sentences.zh: (b) 的问题是没有人为监督，代码可能不完整、设计不当或完全错误。目前我们审查所有代码，因为正确性对我们所做的事情极其重要，但这是有代价的。总的来说，我不知道这里的答案是什么。

sentences.en: Does trust build over time?
sentences.zh: 信任会随着时间的推移而建立吗？

sentences.en: Do the models just get so good we can trust them to make zero mistakes?
sentences.zh: 这些模型是否已经变得足够好，我们可以相信它们会犯零错误？

blocks.html_en:
Part of the value of LLMs and humans is nondeterminism. Pair nondeterministic output with strictly verified results (proper tests) and you can create a useful working system.

Humans can't build a system perfectly, and agents definitely can't. And agents (like humans) will build a different system every time even with the same prompt. Even if it's just trivial differences like array vs linked-list, there are still differences.

The value in having executable code is that it won't change its behavior unless you deliberately modify it. I don't think there are any shortcuts. Models will get smarter and smarter and make fewer mistakes, but you'll still want to produce real source code to execute because it's consistent (you can sell it as a product, set it and forget it, etc.), and most importantly:

running real code is orders of magnitude faster than having an AI either run the process "manually", or have it write the code out multiple times.

sentences.en: Part of the value of LLMs and humans is nondeterminism.
sentences.zh: 法学硕士和人类的部分价值在于不确定性。

sentences.en: Pair nondeterministic output with strictly verified results (proper tests) and you can create a useful working system.Humans can't build a system perfectly, and agents definitely can't.
sentences.zh: 将不确定性输出与严格验证的结果（适当的测试）配对，您可以创建一个有用的工作系统。人类无法完美地构建系统，代理也绝对不能。

sentences.en: And agents (like humans) will build a different system every time even with the same prompt.
sentences.zh: 即使有相同的提示，代理（如人类）每次都会构建不同的系统。

sentences.en: Even if it's just trivial differences like array vs linked-list, there are still differences.The value in having executable code is that it won't change its behavior unless you deliberately modify it.
sentences.zh: 即使只是像数组与链表这样的微小差异，仍然存在差异。拥有可执行代码的价值在于，除非您故意修改它，否则它不会改变其行为。

sentences.en: I don't think there are any shortcuts.
sentences.zh: 我不认为有任何捷径。

sentences.en: Models will get smarter and smarter and make fewer mistakes, but you'll still want to produce real source code to execute because it's consistent (you can sell it as a product, set it and forget it, etc.), and most importantly:running real code is orders of magnitude faster than having an AI either run the process "manually", or have it write the code out multiple times.
sentences.zh: 模型会变得越来越聪明，犯的错误也会越来越少，但你仍然希望生成真正的源代码来执行，因为它是一致的（你可以将其作为产品出售，设置好后就忘记它等），最重要的是：运行真实代码比让人工智能“手动”运行流程或多次编写代码要快几个数量级。

blocks.html_en: I really like what exe.dev is doing. And their philosophy of how cloud services could work is amazing. I have a monthly subscription and it's really cool to just vibe code a little website with Shelley and have it running in minutes. I feel like I have so much fun using their service, and spend so much less time thinking about what things will cost, or what cloud service to use. It's just so much simpler and the friction is so much less compared to other cloud services.

sentences.zh: 他们关于云服务如何运作的理念令人惊叹。

blocks.html_en:
Specs might become one solution for coping with the need to review increased volume of code. A spec is a higher level of abstraction than code, which is a higher level of abstraction than machine code. The industry made the transition to higher-level once, paradigm is changing so it might happen again.

The workflow I imagine is either deriving specs from the conversation or reverse engineering the code to spec, review and edit the spec which should be tighter and much more compressed, then deterministically compile to code. Of course we don't want to be spec-first only, that would be going back waterfall, but doing iterations back and forth.

Now, Claude is not a compiler because it is closed and non-deterministic (they do opaque processing on server, hiding reasoning tokens), but LLMs might be. We refer to a piece of code from npm/pip by name to get some code by downloading it. We then have lockfiles with hashes to ensure integrity. Currently we are vibing it, but in the future we could refer to a piece of code by prompt/spec and getting the code by inferring it. To ensure integrity, the lockfile would be hashes of open weights and inference code (and ironing out implementation details like non-determinism due to GPU scheduling, etc.).

sentences.en: Specs might become one solution for coping with the need to review increased volume of code.
sentences.zh: 规范可能成为应对审查增加的代码量的需要的一种解决方案。

sentences.en: A spec is a higher level of abstraction than code, which is a higher level of abstraction than machine code.
sentences.zh: 规范是比代码更高的抽象级别，而代码是比机器代码更高的抽象级别。

sentences.en: The industry made the transition to higher-level once, paradigm is changing so it might happen again.The workflow I imagine is either deriving specs from the conversation or reverse engineering the code to spec, review and edit the spec which should be tighter and much more compressed, then deterministically compile to code.
sentences.zh: 行业曾经向更高级别过渡，范式正在发生变化，因此可能会再次发生。我想象的工作流程要么从对话中导出规范，要么将代码逆向工程为规范，审查和编辑应该更严格、更压缩的规范，然后确定性地编译为代码。

sentences.en: Of course we don't want to be spec-first only, that would be going back waterfall, but doing iterations back and forth.Now, Claude is not a compiler because it is closed and non-deterministic (they do opaque processing on server, hiding reasoning tokens), but LLMs might be.
sentences.zh: 当然，我们不想只是规范优先，那会回到瀑布，而是来回迭代。现在，Claude 不是编译器，因为它是封闭的且不确定的（它们在服务器上进行不透明处理，隐藏推理令牌），但 LLM 可能是。

sentences.en: We refer to a piece of code from npm/pip by name to get some code by downloading it.
sentences.zh: 我们通过名称引用 npm/pip 中的一段代码，通过下载来获取一些代码。

sentences.en: We then have lockfiles with hashes to ensure integrity.
sentences.zh: 然后我们有带有哈希值的锁文件以确保完整性。

sentences.en: Currently we are vibing it, but in the future we could refer to a piece of code by prompt/spec and getting the code by inferring it.
sentences.zh: 目前我们正在对其进行振动，但将来我们可以通过提示/规范引用一段代码并通过推断来获取代码。

sentences.en: To ensure integrity, the lockfile would be hashes of open weights and inference code (and ironing out implementation details like non-determinism due to GPU scheduling, etc.).
sentences.zh: 为了确保完整性，锁文件将是开放权重和推理代码的哈希值（并消除实现细节，例如由于 GPU 调度而导致的非确定性等）。

blocks.html_en:
It's funny how you describe something very close to what I was attempting to design. It was meant to trickle down from tickets to deterministic code. Data wise, it should be like a pyramid of tickets diluting layer by layer into leaf nodes which were "implementable" as statements in code. I'm not sure if that description makes sense read by someone else.

I think what made sense was envisioning a nanoswarm of LLMs (anticipating ASIC performance) diluting specs into semantic logic nodes, but a conversion from these to deterministic code made the vibe coded experiment come to halt. Your lock approach could be a shortcut to that.

Totally off-topic: it's quite intriguing that you can feel once friction starts building during a design phase. Suddenly everything slows down. I wonder if it's quantifiable and therefore can identify "wrong" design choices made by either humans or LLMs.

(Hoping some claw bot pics this up to finish my idea on my github tix repo in the initial-design branch, as main is empty ~ MPL2)

sentences.zh: 有趣的是，你所描述的东西与我试图设计的东西非常接近。

sentences.en: It was meant to trickle down from tickets to deterministic code.
sentences.zh: 它的目的是从票证渗透到确定性代码。

sentences.en: Data wise, it should be like a pyramid of tickets diluting layer by layer into leaf nodes which were "implementable" as statements in code.
sentences.zh: 从数据角度来说，它应该像一个门票金字塔，逐层稀释为叶节点，这些叶节点可以作为代码中的语句“实现”。

sentences.zh: 我不确定其他人读到的描述是否有意义。我认为有意义的是设想一群 LLM（预期 ASIC 性能）将规范稀释为语义逻辑节点，但从这些到确定性代码的转换使振动编码实验停止了。

sentences.zh: 您的锁定方法可能是实现这一点的捷径。完全偏离主题：在设计阶段一旦出现摩擦，您就会感觉到，这是非常有趣的。

sentences.en: Suddenly everything slows down.
sentences.zh: 突然一切都慢了下来。

blocks.html_en:
This resonates, I can imagine the development process as a cascade: we get business goals and user feedback, product is defined which can be pretty fuzzy, designers define UI, engineering works data models and tech constraints. Inputs at the top can be fuzzy, but each node in the graph applies some intelligence and clarifies it.

Related to the friction you mention, since the flow graph of the context is not preserved when it gets transformed into code, I find LLMs getting confused and making worse changes, because the explicit decisions with good reasons are getting mixed with implicit decisions from LLM surfaced during designing/planning. Sometimes when things change the code should change radically to match the new reality, but LLMs often "play it safe" by accumulating code and adding to tech debt. With a flow graph when change is made in the upper levels, it could propagate and allow decisions in lower levels to be revisited.

sentences.en: This resonates, I can imagine the development process as a cascade: we get business goals and user feedback, product is defined which can be pretty fuzzy, designers define UI, engineering works data models and tech constraints.
sentences.zh: 这引起了共鸣，我可以将开发过程想象为一个级联：我们获得业务目标和用户反馈，产品的定义可能非常模糊，设计师定义 UI，工程数据模型和技术约束。

sentences.en: Inputs at the top can be fuzzy, but each node in the graph applies some intelligence and clarifies it.Related to the friction you mention, since the flow graph of the context is not preserved when it gets transformed into code, I find LLMs getting confused and making worse changes, because the explicit decisions with good reasons are getting mixed with implicit decisions from LLM surfaced during designing/planning.
sentences.zh: 顶部的输入可能是模糊的，但图中的每个节点都应用了一些智能并澄清了它。与您提到的摩擦相关，由于上下文的流程图在转换为代码时不会保留，我发现LLM变得困惑并做出更糟糕的更改，因为有充分理由的显式决策与设计/规划期间浮出水面的LLM的隐式决策混合在一起。

sentences.en: Sometimes when things change the code should change radically to match the new reality, but LLMs often "play it safe" by accumulating code and adding to tech debt.
sentences.zh: 有时，当事情发生变化时，代码应该彻底改变以适应新的现实，但法学硕士经常通过积累代码和增加技术债务来“谨慎行事”。

sentences.en: With a flow graph when change is made in the upper levels, it could propagate and allow decisions in lower levels to be revisited.
sentences.zh: 通过流程图，当上层发生变化时，它可以传播并允许重新审视下层的决策。

sentences.en: This resonates, I can imagine the development process as a cascade: we get business goals and user feedback, product is defined which can be pretty fuzzy, designers define UI, engineering works data models and tech constraints.
sentences.zh: 这引起了共鸣，我可以将开发过程想象为一个级联：我们获得业务目标和用户反馈，产品的定义可能非常模糊，设计师定义 UI，工程数据模型和技术约束。

sentences.en: Inputs at the top can be fuzzy, but each node in the graph applies some intelligence and clarifies it.
sentences.zh: 顶部的输入可能是模糊的，但图中的每个节点都会应用一些智能并使其变得清晰。

blocks.html_en: Related to the friction you mention, since the flow graph of the context is not preserved when it gets transformed into code, I find LLMs getting confused and making worse changes, because the explicit decisions with good reasons are getting mixed with implicit decisions from LLM surfaced during designing/planning. Sometimes when things change the code should change radically to match the new reality, but LLMs often "play it safe" by accumulating code and adding to tech debt. With a flow graph when change is made in the upper levels, it could propagate and allow decisions in lower levels to be revisited.

sentences.en: Related to the friction you mention, since the flow graph of the context is not preserved when it gets transformed into code, I find LLMs getting confused and making worse changes, because the explicit decisions with good reasons are getting mixed with implicit decisions from LLM surfaced during designing/planning.
sentences.zh: 与你提到的摩擦相关，由于上下文的流程图在转换为代码时不会被保留，我发现法学硕士会感到困惑并做出更糟糕的改变，因为有充分理由的明确决策与设计/规划期间出现的法学硕士的隐式决策混合在一起。

sentences.en: Sometimes when things change the code should change radically to match the new reality, but LLMs often "play it safe" by accumulating code and adding to tech debt.
sentences.zh: 有时，当事情发生变化时，代码应该彻底改变以适应新的现实，但法学硕士经常通过积累代码和增加技术债务来“谨慎行事”。

sentences.en: With a flow graph when change is made in the upper levels, it could propagate and allow decisions in lower levels to be revisited.
sentences.zh: 通过流程图，当上层发生变化时，它可以传播并允许重新审视下层的决策。

blocks.html_en:
This might work if we have completely new harnesses for this, because the current one’s intentionally introduce non-reproducible context and tools.

The current way we’re doing SDD is extremely pointless. You reach a decision point that is under specified, make a decision, update the spec and continue the implementation. There’s no guarantee that the implementation followed from the updated spec, and not from the decision.

sentences.en: There’s no guarantee that the implementation followed from the updated spec, and not from the decision.
sentences.zh: 无法保证实施遵循更新的规范，而不是决策。

blocks.html_en: I agree, another issue I have with existing SDD frameworks I've seen like spec-kit or openspec, is they seem to miss the mark by coupling specification (what the software should be) and execution (how to get there). If the description of execution is needed, it means the spec is weak. As a rule of thumb if a framework defines execution concepts like tasks or tickets, that is a red flag for me.

sentences.en: I agree, another issue I have with existing SDD frameworks I've seen like spec-kit or openspec, is they seem to miss the mark by coupling specification (what the software should be) and execution (how to get there).
sentences.zh: 我同意，我所见过的现有 SDD 框架（如spec-kit 或 openspec）的另一个问题是，它们似乎在耦合规范（软件应该是什么）和执行（如何到达那里）方面没有达到目标。

sentences.en: If the description of execution is needed, it means the spec is weak.
sentences.zh: 如果需要执行的描述，则说明规范很弱。

sentences.en: As a rule of thumb if a framework defines execution concepts like tasks or tickets, that is a red flag for me.
sentences.zh: 根据经验，如果框架定义了任务或票证等执行概念，这对我来说是一个危险信号。

blocks.html_en: prompts are not specs because most often, prompts are underspecified. You can prompt fizzbuzz well enough for it to be functional, but not ffmpeg. So the distinction is less about Claude's capability, more about the quality of the input. Garbage in garbage out

sentences.en: prompts are not specs because most often, prompts are underspecified.
sentences.zh: 提示不是规范，因为大多数情况下，提示未指定。

sentences.en: You can prompt fizzbuzz well enough for it to be functional, but not ffmpeg.
sentences.zh: 您可以很好地提示 fizzbuzz 使其发挥作用，但 ffmpeg 不行。

sentences.en: So the distinction is less about Claude's capability, more about the quality of the input.
sentences.zh: 因此，区别并不是克劳德的能力，而是输入的质量。

sentences.en: Garbage in garbage out
sentences.zh: 垃圾进垃圾出

blocks.html_en:
I think something can be considered a spec to some degree if outcome was derived from it.

It might underspecify, but that is just a quality of the spec. And as long as there is any discretion at implementation time, then the spec necessarily underspecifies to some degree. The goal of a spec is often to be clear on key directional concerns. Consider RFCs.

There's a trade-off between specification and implementation discretion. But if you think about starting at one end ("an internet forum") and then incrementally expanding the specifications ("it has topics with many posts in a linear paginated list"), there's some point where everything you care about is in the spec, and the rest is left to implementor discretion.

That's what I'd consider a good spec and a good LLM prompt.

sentences.en: I think something can be considered a spec to some degree if outcome was derived from it.It might underspecify, but that is just a quality of the spec.
sentences.zh: 我认为，如果结果是从某件事中得出的，那么在某种程度上可以将其视为规范。它可能不够规范，但这只是规范的质量。

sentences.en: And as long as there is any discretion at implementation time, then the spec necessarily underspecifies to some degree.
sentences.zh: 只要在实施时有任何自由裁量权，那么规范就必然在某种程度上未指定。

sentences.en: The goal of a spec is often to be clear on key directional concerns.
sentences.zh: 规范的目标通常是明确关键方向问题。

sentences.en: Consider RFCs.There's a trade-off between specification and implementation discretion.
sentences.zh: 考虑 RFC。规范和实现自由裁量权之间需要权衡。

sentences.en: But if you think about starting at one end ("an internet forum") and then incrementally expanding the specifications ("it has topics with many posts in a linear paginated list"), there's some point where everything you care about is in the spec, and the rest is left to implementor discretion.That's what I'd consider a good spec and a good LLM prompt.
sentences.zh: 但是，如果您考虑从一端（“互联网论坛”）开始，然后逐步扩展规范（“它的主题在线性分页列表中包含许多帖子”），那么在某种程度上，您关心的所有内容都在规范中，其余的则留给实现者自行决定。这就是我认为良好的规范和良好的 LLM 提示。

blocks.html_en: You can reference other specs in a prompt. Video codecs are well specified with hundreds of pages of specs, they are probably in some form in the weights already, but you could also reference them and hash them as inputs directly to create a functional alternative to ffmpeg.

sentences.en: Video codecs are well specified with hundreds of pages of specs, they are probably in some form in the weights already, but you could also reference them and hash them as inputs directly to create a functional alternative to ffmpeg.

blocks.html_en:
> A spec is a higher level of abstraction than code, which is a higher level of abstraction than machine code

No it’s not.

Just like a quick doodle is not an higher level representation of the Mona Lisa. Sure for someones that knows the latter, it can suggest it. Or for someone that doesn’t know it, it may provide some basis of conversation. But it’s not the real thing. You can’t provide a doodle of something and expect an artist that hasn’t seen it to paint it.

Doodling’s value is that it lets you iterate on ideas without the accidental constraints that comes with the implementation (choosing paints, finding references materials, deciding colors,…). Some necessary choices are kept for later, while you decide on the most important ones.

So a spec is useful in designing software, but it’s severely lacking in implementing it. And the decisions made in implementation are as important as the ones made in the design. Even more after a while in production.

sentences.en: > A spec is a higher level of abstraction than code, which is a higher level of abstraction than machine codeNo it’s not.Just like a quick doodle is not an higher level representation of the Mona Lisa.

sentences.en: Or for someone that doesn’t know it, it may provide some basis of conversation.
sentences.zh: 或者对于不知道的人来说，它可能提供一些对话基础。

sentences.en: But it’s not the real thing.
sentences.zh: 但这不是真实的事情。

sentences.zh: 你不能提供一些东西的涂鸦，然后指望没有见过它的艺术家来绘制它。涂鸦的价值在于，它可以让你迭代想法，而不会受到实施过程中的意外限制（选择颜料、查找参考材料、决定颜色……）。

sentences.en: Some necessary choices are kept for later, while you decide on the most important ones.So a spec is useful in designing software, but it’s severely lacking in implementing it.
sentences.zh: 一些必要的选择会保留下来，供以后决定最重要的选择。因此，规范在设计软件时很有用，但在实现软件时却严重缺乏。

sentences.en: And the decisions made in implementation are as important as the ones made in the design.
sentences.zh: 实施过程中做出的决策与设计中做出的决策同样重要。

sentences.en: Even more after a while in production.

blocks.html_en:
> Claude wasn’t just a compiler here. I never handed off a task and let an agent make a bunch of decisions in order to reduce it to practice.

> I’d say that, in all the ways that matter, I understand the code.

I think the dissonance here is really important, and not a bad thing at all. A lot of the decision _were_ handed off the the AI, but they weren't the decisions the author cared about. This is a big selling point of AI! If something is doable with a computer, it’ll figure it out. 30 minutes and 200m tokens later, it’ll take any idea and declare “the feature is fully implemented.”

The hard part is figuring out where to inject that friction, so you can see where it's making decisions for you that matter. The author approached this by incrementally building the thing, reviewing and poking and prodding at every step. A week of attention following a bunch of design discussions is fast, but that's still not trivially cheap.

I want to see us talk more about the decision exhaust of agents, because the better the models get, the more decisions we'll want them to make.

I wrote a bit more here: https://tern.sh/blog/compiler-never-says-no/

blocks.html_en:
> I read a vanishingly small amount of the actual code.

This sentiment kind of saddens me. I'm all for burning tokens to write throwaway code just to prototype a solution, but I don't get not reading (or at least familiarizing yourself with) the code that you will deploy to prod.

sentences.en: > I read a vanishingly small amount of the actual code.This sentiment kind of saddens me.
sentences.zh: > 我读了极少的实际代码。这种情绪让我有点悲伤。

sentences.en: I'm all for burning tokens to write throwaway code just to prototype a solution, but I don't get not reading (or at least familiarizing yourself with) the code that you will deploy to prod.
sentences.zh: 我完全赞成燃烧代币来编写一次性代码只是为了构建解决方案的原型，但我不会不阅读（或至少熟悉）您将部署到产品的代码。

blocks.html_en:
> But our VMs start fast, so fast that even if we created the DNS entries before creating the VM, our users still had to sit around waiting for DNS to propagate, which occasionally took minutes, not seconds.

To my (limited) understanding, this is not a good idea, and is an unfixable problem from the server side. Companies, VPNs or ISPs or routers, often use their own DNS servers, and those can have caching logic, which means it doesn't matter how fast your own DNS implementation is, as the users lookup request wont hit your DNS server, it'll hit an intermediate cache.

sentences.en: > But our VMs start fast, so fast that even if we created the DNS entries before creating the VM, our users still had to sit around waiting for DNS to propagate, which occasionally took minutes, not seconds.To my (limited) understanding, this is not a good idea, and is an unfixable problem from the server side.

blocks.html_en:
Presumably these are new subdomains, if a caching DNS resolvers never saw the domain yet, it would simply query the authoritative server.

There's a small nuance here that DNS spec also caches negative (NXDOMAIN) results, but the TTL of those is controllable by the SOA record on the domain that contains it.

exe.xyz has a fairly short 10 second negative TTL, so even if you tried to resolve it before it was set up in the authoritative DNS, it wouldn't stay negative-cached very long (though some DNS caches put a lower bound on the TTL and cache it for longer)

> dig +short soa exe.xyz

ns1.exe.dev. hostmaster.exe.dev. 1854440 86400 7200 1209600 10

sentences.en: Presumably these are new subdomains, if a caching DNS resolvers never saw the domain yet, it would simply query the authoritative server.There's a small nuance here that DNS spec also caches negative (NXDOMAIN) results, but the TTL of those is controllable by the SOA record on the domain that contains it.exe.xyz has a fairly short 10 second negative TTL, so even if you tried to resolve it before it was set up in the authoritative DNS, it wouldn't stay negative-cached very long (though some DNS caches put a lower bound on the TTL and cache it for longer)> dig +short soa exe.xyzns1.exe.dev.
sentences.zh: 据推测，这些是新的子域，如果缓存 DNS 解析器从未见过该域，它只会查询权威服务器。这里有一个小细微差别，DNS 规范也缓存负 (NXDOMAIN) 结果，但这些结果的 TTL 可以由包含它的域上的 SOA 记录控制。exe.xyz 的负 TTL 相当短，为 10 秒，因此，即使您在权威 DNS 中设置它之前尝试解析它，它不会保持负缓存很长时间（尽管某些 DNS 缓存对 TTL 设定了下限并缓存更长时间）> dig +short soa exe.xyzns1.exe.dev。

sentences.en: hostmaster.exe.dev.

blocks.html_en: Usually when you create the VM, it gets its own DNS subdomain that looks like `my-new-vm-name.exe.xyz`. (I'm not affiliated with exe.dev, I just use their product). So I doubt there would be any cached requests for that particular subdomain before the VM is created.

sentences.zh: 因此，我怀疑在创建虚拟机之前是否会有针对该特定子域的任何缓存请求。

blocks.html_en:
NXDOMAIN results and other errors have their own TTL value though, so if someone queries too early in the process you could be waiting a little while for their cache to check again. The value for this is set in the SOA record for your DNS zone.

For example, example.org/example.com seems to have TTLs set at 300s (5 minutes) for A records, but the negative caching value is 1800s (30 minutes). If your VM domain is set the same way, you could see a premature lookup causing issues for 30 minutes instead of just 5. Even the 5 minutes could be a pain if your VM spin-up process expects to be able to lookup via the name very early and will fall over if it can't.

sentences.en: NXDOMAIN results and other errors have their own TTL value though, so if someone queries too early in the process you could be waiting a little while for their cache to check again.
sentences.zh: 不过，NXDOMAIN 结果和其他错误都有自己的 TTL 值，因此，如果有人在此过程中过早查询，您可能需要等待一段时间才能再次检查他们的缓存。

sentences.en: The value for this is set in the SOA record for your DNS zone.For example, example.org/example.com seems to have TTLs set at 300s (5 minutes) for A records, but the negative caching value is 1800s (30 minutes).
sentences.zh: 该值在 DNS 区域的 SOA 记录中设置。例如，example.org/example.com 似乎将 A 记录的 TTL 设置为 300 秒（5 分钟），但负缓存值为 1800 秒（30 分钟）。

sentences.en: If your VM domain is set the same way, you could see a premature lookup causing issues for 30 minutes instead of just 5.
sentences.zh: 如果您的 VM 域以相同方式设置，您可能会看到过早查找导致问题持续 30 分钟，而不是仅仅 5 分钟。

sentences.en: Even the 5 minutes could be a pain if your VM spin-up process expects to be able to lookup via the name very early and will fall over if it can't.
sentences.zh: 如果您的虚拟机启动进程希望能够尽早通过名称进行查找，并且如果不能的话就会失败，那么即使 5 分钟也可能会很痛苦。

blocks.html_en:
I always don't know enough about DNS. This is great stuff.

If exe.dev is running their own DNS zone, then wouldn't they be setting the expiry value in the SOA record themselves? So they could set it to something short, and secondary servers are supposed to honor it, right?

sentences.en: I always don't know enough about DNS.
sentences.zh: 我一直对DNS了解不够。

sentences.en: This is great stuff.If exe.dev is running their own DNS zone, then wouldn't they be setting the expiry value in the SOA record themselves?
sentences.zh: 这是很棒的东西。如果 exe.dev 正在运行他们自己的 DNS 区域，那么他们不会自己在 SOA 记录中设置到期值吗？

sentences.en: So they could set it to something short, and secondary servers are supposed to honor it, right?
sentences.zh: 所以他们可以将其设置为较短的值，并且辅助服务器应该遵守它，对吗？
