blocks.html_en:
> wouldn't they be setting the expiry value in the SOA record themselves?

If they thought about it. But it is something that even people relatively experienced in DNS matters forget about until it bites them and they've spent ages diagnosing the initially odd looking problems caused.

> To my (limited) understanding, this is not a good idea, and is an unfixable problem from the server side. Companies, VPNs or ISPs or routers, often use their own DNS servers, and those can have caching logic, which means it doesn't matter how fast your own DNS implementation is, as the users lookup request wont hit your DNS server, it'll hit an intermediate cache.

All caches should respect the TTL of DNS records, no matter what they are, though there are some interesting bugs out there.

Firstly the human bugs: a lot of people assume switching DNS is always fast, but they haven't had the TTLs property of a DNS entry explained to them so assume that if the change is working here then it is going to be working <there>, <there>, and <everywhere-else> too. The default TTL values are a mix, it used to be that 24 hours was common though these days four hours or less, sometimes even one hour, is what you are more likely to see even for defaults. Whatever value your entries have though, that doesn't mean a fixed × hour window that it will switch at the end of. If the TTL is four hours your local cache last checked three hours ago but mine last checked one hour ago, a change will take an hour for you to see and three for me to see. A cache that has not recently looked up the entry will see it immediately next time it is asked - this is why some assume it is always instant (they add a new sub-domain record and it works instantly because no one has ever requested an address for that name before, for instance, and assume this is how it will always work).

Then there are coded bugs. TBH these days I ignore those: they are rare enough that if you are caring about that sort of edge case a lot then there are a lot of other weeds you'll be worrying about too and you'll never get anything done. For my domains I mostly have everything set to five minutes (300 seconds) as they are rarely referenced and DNS infrastructure is not a high-power service these days. Some would suggest that using such a low default is unfriendly to DNS caches and the root servers, but my names are very rarely referenced outside my network/vpn where my own DNS authoritative servers are what get directly queried anyway so that isn't going to matter. example.{org|com} and some sizable companies use 300s too. Why five minutes and not less? There used to be a very common caching DNS server that would ignore anything lower than 300 seconds and apply its own default (14,400 seconds, four hours, IIRC) instead. This goes back a couple of decades though, if anyone is still running that they deserve some DNS lookup failures! If you are wanting to use longer values normally, just remember to lower your TTL a time before any planned change so the change propagates ASAP and raise them back up after. One extra thing to be aware of with short TTLs is an outage affecting all your authoritative DNS servers may cause less friendly errors in many cases than getting an out of date address would result in, so make sure your DNS servers are stable and have few (preferably zero) shared potential points of failure.

sentences.en: > To my (limited) understanding, this is not a good idea, and is an unfixable problem from the server side.
sentences.zh: > 以我（有限）的理解，这不是一个好主意，并且是服务器端无法修复的问题。

sentences.en: Companies, VPNs or ISPs or routers, often use their own DNS servers, and those can have caching logic, which means it doesn't matter how fast your own DNS implementation is, as the users lookup request wont hit your DNS server, it'll hit an intermediate cache.All caches should respect the TTL of DNS records, no matter what they are, though there are some interesting bugs out there.Firstly the human bugs: a lot of people assume switching DNS is always fast, but they haven't had the TTLs property of a DNS entry explained to them so assume that if the change is working here then it is going to be working <there>, <there>, and <everywhere-else> too.
sentences.zh: 公司、VPN、ISP 或路由器经常使用自己的 DNS 服务器，并且这些服务器可以具有缓存逻辑，这意味着您自己的 DNS 实现速度有多快并不重要，因为用户的查找请求不会到达您的 DNS 服务器，它会到达中间缓存。所有缓存都应该尊重 DNS 记录的 TTL，无论它们是什么，尽管存在一些有趣的错误。首先是人为错误：很多人认为切换 DNS 总是很快，但他们并没有向他们解释了 DNS 条目的 TTL 属性，因此假设如果更改在这里起作用，那么它也将在 <there>、<there> 和 <everywhere-else> 上起作用。

sentences.en: The default TTL values are a mix, it used to be that 24 hours was common though these days four hours or less, sometimes even one hour, is what you are more likely to see even for defaults.
sentences.zh: 默认 TTL 值是一个混合值，过去 24 小时很常见，但现在即使是默认值，您也更有可能看到 4 小时或更短时间，有时甚至是 1 小时。

sentences.en: Whatever value your entries have though, that doesn't mean a fixed × hour window that it will switch at the end of.
sentences.zh: 无论您的条目有什么价值，这并不意味着它会在结束时切换固定的 × 小时窗口。

sentences.en: If the TTL is four hours your local cache last checked three hours ago but mine last checked one hour ago, a change will take an hour for you to see and three for me to see.
sentences.zh: 如果 TTL 是四个小时，您的本地缓存上次检查是在三小时前，但我的上次检查是在一小时前，那么您需要一个小时才能看到更改，而我需要三个小时才能看到更改。

sentences.en: A cache that has not recently looked up the entry will see it immediately next time it is asked - this is why some assume it is always instant (they add a new sub-domain record and it works instantly because no one has ever requested an address for that name before, for instance, and assume this is how it will always work).Then there are coded bugs.
sentences.zh: 最近没有查找过该条目的缓存将在下次被询问时立即看到它 - 这就是为什么有些人认为它总是即时的（他们添加一个新的子域记录并且它会立即工作，因为例如，之前没有人请求过该名称的地址，并假设这就是它总是工作的方式）。然后存在编码错误。

sentences.en: TBH these days I ignore those: they are rare enough that if you are caring about that sort of edge case a lot then there are a lot of other weeds you'll be worrying about too and you'll never get anything done.
sentences.zh: 说实话，这些天我忽略了这些：它们非常罕见，如果你非常关心这种边缘情况，那么你也会担心很多其他杂草，而且你永远不会完成任何事情。

sentences.en: For my domains I mostly have everything set to five minutes (300 seconds) as they are rarely referenced and DNS infrastructure is not a high-power service these days.
sentences.zh: 对于我的域，我大多将所有内容设置为五分钟（300 秒），因为它们很少被引用，而且如今 DNS 基础设施并不是一个高功率服务。

sentences.en: Some would suggest that using such a low default is unfriendly to DNS caches and the root servers, but my names are very rarely referenced outside my network/vpn where my own DNS authoritative servers are what get directly queried anyway so that isn't going to matter.
sentences.zh: 有些人会建议使用如此低的默认值对 DNS 缓存和根服务器不友好，但我的名字很少在我的网络/VPN 之外被引用，其中我自己的 DNS 权威服务器无论如何都会被直接查询，所以这并不重要。

sentences.en: example.{org|com} and some sizable companies use 300s too.
sentences.zh: example.{org|com} 和一些大公司也使用 300。

sentences.en: Why five minutes and not less?
sentences.zh: 为什么是五分钟而不是更少？

sentences.en: There used to be a very common caching DNS server that would ignore anything lower than 300 seconds and apply its own default (14,400 seconds, four hours, IIRC) instead.
sentences.zh: 曾经有一个非常常见的缓存 DNS 服务器会忽略任何低于 300 秒的内容并应用其自己的默认值（14,400 秒，四小时，IIRC）。

sentences.en: This goes back a couple of decades though, if anyone is still running that they deserve some DNS lookup failures!
sentences.zh: 不过，这可以追溯到几十年前，如果有人仍在运行，他们就应该遭受一些 DNS 查找失败！

sentences.en: If you are wanting to use longer values normally, just remember to lower your TTL a time before any planned change so the change propagates ASAP and raise them back up after.
sentences.zh: 如果您想正常使用更长的值，只需记住在任何计划更改之前降低 TTL 一次，以便更改尽快传播并在之后将其恢复。

sentences.en: One extra thing to be aware of with short TTLs is an outage affecting all your authoritative DNS servers may cause less friendly errors in many cases than getting an out of date address would result in, so make sure your DNS servers are stable and have few (preferably zero) shared potential points of failure.
sentences.zh: 对于短 TTL，需要注意的另一件事是，影响所有权威 DNS 服务器的中断在许多情况下可能会导致比获取过期地址更不友好的错误，因此请确保您的 DNS 服务器稳定并且具有很少（最好是零）的共享潜在故障点。

sentences.en: If you are wanting to use longer values normally, just remember to lower your TTL a time before any planned change so the change propagates ASAP and raise them back up after.
sentences.zh: 如果您想正常使用更长的值，只需记住在任何计划更改之前降低 TTL 一次，以便更改尽快传播并在之后将其恢复。

sentences.en: One extra thing to be aware of with short TTLs is an outage affecting all your authoritative DNS servers may cause less friendly errors in many cases than getting an out of date address would result in, so make sure your DNS servers are stable and have few (preferably zero) shared potential points of failure.
sentences.zh: 对于短 TTL，需要注意的另一件事是，影响所有权威 DNS 服务器的中断在许多情况下可能会导致比获取过期地址更不友好的错误，因此请确保您的 DNS 服务器稳定并且具有很少（最好是零）的共享潜在故障点。

blocks.html_en:
A compiler is an algorithm and Claude isn't*. A compiler almost never produces a wrong output, even when compiling an extremely complicated program. But a compiler must be clearly defined and is limited to input/commands it's defined for. A compiler will "correctly" process input into unintuitive output, but that's not always what the user intended (e.g. omitting large sections of code that are undefined behavior). Claude makes mistakes, but can process any input/command expressible in training data, and has more potential to infer when something is unintentional.

Tools like Claude are great at building algorithms, then tests to verify them. The algorithms they build aren't just compilers, but any software that can be verified. Even though Claude may not be a compiler, it has the potential to (at least if it improves) create production-grade software that can be verified, like other compilers. But maybe not good UX without human input (expanded to any subjective experience, like video games).

* Not a "source language to target language" deductive algorithm. Technically Claude is an algorithm to predict the next token, but acting as a compiler or anything else it's an inductive heuristic, because it guesses (https://stackoverflow.com/q/2334225)

sentences.en: A compiler is an algorithm and Claude isn't*.
sentences.zh: 编译器是一种算法，而 Claude 不是*。

sentences.en: A compiler almost never produces a wrong output, even when compiling an extremely complicated program.
sentences.zh: 即使编译极其复杂的程序，编译器也几乎不会产生错误的输出。

sentences.en: But a compiler must be clearly defined and is limited to input/commands it's defined for.
sentences.zh: 但是编译器必须明确定义，并且仅限于其定义的输入/命令。

sentences.en: A compiler will "correctly" process input into unintuitive output, but that's not always what the user intended (e.g. omitting large sections of code that are undefined behavior).
sentences.zh: 编译器将“正确”地将输入处理为不直观的输出，但这并不总是用户想要的（例如，省略大部分未定义行为的代码）。

sentences.en: Claude makes mistakes, but can process any input/command expressible in training data, and has more potential to infer when something is unintentional.Tools like Claude are great at building algorithms, then tests to verify them.
sentences.zh: Claude 会犯错误，但可以处理训练数据中可表达的任何输入/命令，并且更有潜力推断出无意的情况。像 Claude 这样的工具非常擅长构建算法，然后进行测试来验证它们。

sentences.en: The algorithms they build aren't just compilers, but any software that can be verified.
sentences.zh: 他们构建的算法不仅仅是编译器，而是任何可以验证的软件。

sentences.en: Even though Claude may not be a compiler, it has the potential to (at least if it improves) create production-grade software that can be verified, like other compilers.
sentences.zh: 尽管 Claude 可能不是一个编译器，但它有潜力（至少如果它有所改进）创建可验证的生产级软件，就像其他编译器一样。

sentences.en: But maybe not good UX without human input (expanded to any subjective experience, like video games).* Not a "source language to target language" deductive algorithm.
sentences.zh: 但如果没有人工输入，用户体验可能不会很好（扩展到任何主观体验，例如视频游戏）。*不是“源语言到目标语言”的演绎算法。

sentences.en: Technically Claude is an algorithm to predict the next token, but acting as a compiler or anything else it's an inductive heuristic, because it guesses (https://stackoverflow.com/q/2334225)
sentences.zh: 从技术上讲，Claude 是一种预测下一个标记的算法，但作为编译器或其他任何东西，它是一种归纳启发式算法，因为它会猜测（https://stackoverflow.com/q/2334225）

blocks.html_en:
> A compiler will "correctly" process input into unintuitive output, but that's not always what the user intended

Importantly, this behavior is deterministic. If the compiler takes your code and spits out something you did not expect, it will always spit out that same result, given the same input. Once you figure out what went wrong, you've solved that problem.

Generative AI is intentionally not deterministic, and that means you're going to solve the same issues -- hallucinations, primarily -- over and over again.

sentences.en: > A compiler will "correctly" process input into unintuitive output, but that's not always what the user intendedImportantly, this behavior is deterministic.
sentences.zh: > 编译器将“正确”地将输入处理为不直观的输出，但这并不总是用户想要的。重要的是，这种行为是确定性的。

sentences.en: If the compiler takes your code and spits out something you did not expect, it will always spit out that same result, given the same input.
sentences.zh: 如果编译器获取您的代码并输出一些您没有预料到的内容，那么在给定相同的输入的情况下，它总是会输出相同的结果。

sentences.en: Once you figure out what went wrong, you've solved that problem.Generative AI is intentionally not deterministic, and that means you're going to solve the same issues -- hallucinations, primarily -- over and over again.
sentences.zh: 一旦你弄清楚出了什么问题，你就解决了这个问题。生成式人工智能故意不具有确定性，这意味着你将一次又一次地解决同样的问题——主要是幻觉。

blocks.html_en:
Determinism isn't the main hurdle. It would be a moderate effort to engineer a deterministic claude that would be no more useful than the current one.

The key characteristic of a compiler is that it takes a source language with a well-defined meaning to a target language with a well-defined meaning.

The reason claude fails to be a compiler is that its input language (english) can't be precisely defined. (And even if that were possible, it would very often fail to preserve the meaning when "compiling")

sentences.en: Determinism isn't the main hurdle.
sentences.zh: 决定论并不是主要障碍。

sentences.en: It would be a moderate effort to engineer a deterministic claude that would be no more useful than the current one.The key characteristic of a compiler is that it takes a source language with a well-defined meaning to a target language with a well-defined meaning.The reason claude fails to be a compiler is that its input language (english) can't be precisely defined.
sentences.zh: 设计一个确定性的克劳德将是一项适度的努力，它不会比当前的有用。编译器的关键特征是它将具有明确定义的含义的源语言转换为具有明确定义的含义的目标语言。克劳德无法成为编译器的原因是它的输入语言（英语）无法精确定义。

sentences.en: (And even if that were possible, it would very often fail to preserve the meaning when "compiling")
sentences.zh: （即使这是可能的，在“编译”时也常常无法保留含义）

blocks.html_en: AI could be deterministic, only it requires sacrificing performance and nobody cares enough. Although algorithms are more predictable, and you can follow an algorithm's steps (even a complex algorithm can be broken into large sequences of steps, which may be broken further).

sentences.en: AI could be deterministic, only it requires sacrificing performance and nobody cares enough.
sentences.zh: 人工智能可以是确定性的，只是它需要牺牲性能，而且没有人足够关心。

blocks.html_en:
Determinism is the wrong thing to worry about. As you say an LLM could theoretically be deterministic.

Chaos (prompt instability) is the problem that isn’t solvable if you want to treat an LLM like a compiler by doing something like checking in the prompts and rebuilding the code each time.

In general if you change a single line of source code, a compiler will not produce a completely different program. If you change a single word in a prompt, you will get output that likely doesn’t match a single line of the original output.

Chaos is what leads to unpredictability, and there’s no solution to this on the horizon.

sentences.en: Determinism is the wrong thing to worry about.
sentences.zh: 担心决定论是错误的。

sentences.en: As you say an LLM could theoretically be deterministic.Chaos (prompt instability) is the problem that isn’t solvable if you want to treat an LLM like a compiler by doing something like checking in the prompts and rebuilding the code each time.In general if you change a single line of source code, a compiler will not produce a completely different program.
sentences.zh: 正如你所说，LLM理论上是确定性的。如果你想像编译器一样对待LLM，每次都检查提示并重建代码，那么混乱（提示不稳定）是无法解决的问题。一般来说，如果你更改一行源代码，编译器不会生成完全不同的程序。

sentences.en: If you change a single word in a prompt, you will get output that likely doesn’t match a single line of the original output.Chaos is what leads to unpredictability, and there’s no solution to this on the horizon.
sentences.zh: 如果您更改提示中的单个单词，您将得到的输出可能与原始输出的一行不匹配。混乱是导致不可预测性的原因，而且目前还没有解决方案。

blocks.html_en:
Determinism is the wrong thing to worry about. As you say an LLM could theoretically be deterministic.

Chaos (prompt instability) is the problem that isn’t solvable if you want to treat an LLM like a compiler by doing something like checking in the prompts and rebuilding the code each time.

In general if you change a single line of source code, a compiler will not produce a completely different program. If you change a single word in a prompt, you will get output that likely doesn’t match a single line of the original output.

Chaos is what leads to unpredictability, and there’s no solution to this on the horizon.

sentences.en: Determinism is the wrong thing to worry about.
sentences.zh: 担心决定论是错误的。

blocks.html_en: I think you're correct, I'll just point out that compilers are also chaotic today and it is often accepted. A small change to source program can lead to big change in performance. It's especially apparent in high-level and declarative languages - SQL or anything with GC.

sentences.zh: 这在高级声明性语言（SQL 或任何带有 GC 的语言）中尤其明显。

With respect to performance that’s true. But I’ve probably spent thousands of hours in my career on performance issues caused by small SQL changes.

And LLM chaos is unimaginably worse. A single white space can result in a program that has zero overlap with the original.

blocks.html_en:
“Generative AI is intentionally not deterministic, and that means you're going to solve the same issues -- hallucinations, primarily -- over and over again.”

I see it differently. Sure there are still some hallucinations (but less and less as we progress on), but I see the AI-is-not-deterministic characteristic as a good thing, in the same way that humans and human designers not being deterministic is a good thing. Imagine giving a project/task to a human, and it was completed exactly in the same way with exactly the same outputs as another human that the project was tasked to. Would that be better than each human having varying outcomes of the project? I think most would agree the answer is no.

sentences.en: “Generative AI is intentionally not deterministic, and that means you're going to solve the same issues -- hallucinations, primarily -- over and over again.”I see it differently.
sentences.zh: “生成式人工智能故意不具有确定性，这意味着你将一次又一次地解决同样的问题——主要是幻觉。”我有不同的看法。

sentences.en: Sure there are still some hallucinations (but less and less as we progress on), but I see the AI-is-not-deterministic characteristic as a good thing, in the same way that humans and human designers not being deterministic is a good thing.
sentences.zh: 当然，仍然存在一些幻觉（但随着我们的进展越来越少），但我认为人工智能非确定性特征是一件好事，就像人类和人类设计师不具有确定性是一件好事一样。

sentences.en: Imagine giving a project/task to a human, and it was completed exactly in the same way with exactly the same outputs as another human that the project was tasked to.
sentences.zh: 想象一下，将一个项目/任务交给一个人，它的完成方式与该项目所分配的另一个人完全相同，输出也完全相同。

sentences.en: Would that be better than each human having varying outcomes of the project?
sentences.zh: 这会比每个人都有不同的项目结果更好吗？

sentences.en: I think most would agree the answer is no.
sentences.zh: 我想大多数人都会同意答案是否定的。

blocks.html_en:
In a production-grade compiler like LLVM they are rare enough that it compiles giant projects like Linux and Chromium without detectable issues.

Maybe eventually Claude can, since it can write tests, but compilers / (deductive) algorithms have other advantages like efficiency and predictability.

sentences.en: In a production-grade compiler like LLVM they are rare enough that it compiles giant projects like Linux and Chromium without detectable issues.Maybe eventually Claude can, since it can write tests, but compilers / (deductive) algorithms have other advantages like efficiency and predictability.
sentences.zh: 在像 LLVM 这样的生产级编译器中，它们非常罕见，以至于它可以编译像 Linux 和 Chromium 这样的大型项目而不会出现可检测到的问题。也许 Claude 最终可以，因为它可以编写测试，但编译器/（演绎）算法还有其他优势，例如效率和可预测性。

blocks.html_en:
Have a go at writing something in 1975's C.

x=-5 Is not x = -5

There's no unsigned int.

It's a better comparison to the usability of Claude than you might think. It's doable, but hardly ideal.

x=-5 Is not x = -5

There's no unsigned int.

It's a better comparison to the usability of Claude than you might think. It's doable, but hardly ideal.

blocks.html_en:
> A compiler is an algorithm and Claude isn't

"Algorithm" is not a word with a definition, so I can't say that you're wrong, but I struggle to see how you conceptualize "algorithm" in a way that does include a compiler but does not include a language model.

I think the distinction you're trying to draw is better captured by comparing inductive and deductive program synthesis.

sentences.en: > A compiler is an algorithm and Claude isn't"Algorithm" is not a word with a definition, so I can't say that you're wrong, but I struggle to see how you conceptualize "algorithm" in a way that does include a compiler but does not include a language model.I think the distinction you're trying to draw is better captured by comparing inductive and deductive program synthesis.
sentences.zh: >编译器是一种算法，而克劳德不是“算法”，不是一个有定义的词，所以我不能说你错了，但我很难理解你如何以包含编译器但不包含语言模型的方式概念化“算法”。我认为通过比较归纳和演绎程序综合可以更好地捕捉你试图绘制的区别。

blocks.html_en: In computer science, one definition of algorithm is basically any program that runs on a turing machine. By that definition, any LLM is an algorithm.

sentences.en: By that definition, any LLM is an algorithm.
sentences.zh: 根据这个定义，任何法学硕士都是一种算法。

blocks.html_en:
A compiler is a deterministic algorithm. Clause is not.

Of course Claude is an algorithm, but it's a very vague, fuzzy and complex one, where you have no way to determine what's going on inside, and no way to know up front what's going to come out.

sentences.en: A compiler is a deterministic algorithm.
sentences.zh: 编译器是一种确定性算法。

sentences.en: Clause is not.Of course Claude is an algorithm, but it's a very vague, fuzzy and complex one, where you have no way to determine what's going on inside, and no way to know up front what's going to come out.
sentences.zh: Clause 不是。当然 Claude 是一种算法，但它是一种非常模糊、模糊和复杂的算法，你无法确定内部发生了什么，也无法预先知道会出现什么。

blocks.html_en:
Rogers "Theory of Recursive Functions and Effective Computability", page 1, emphasis is the author's:

§ 1.1 The informal notion of algorithm

In this chapter we give a formal (i.e., mathematically exact) characterization of recursive function. The concept is basic for the remainder of the book. It is one way of making precise the informal mathematical notion of function computable "by algorithm" or "by effective procedure". In this section, as a preliminary to the formal characterization, we discuss certain aspects of the informal notions of algorithm and function computable by algorithm as they occur in mathematics.

I don't understand why you people act like you're stumped by literally, literally page 1 of computer science.

sentences.en: Rogers "Theory of Recursive Functions and Effective Computability", page 1, emphasis is the author's:§ 1.1 The informal notion of algorithmIn this chapter we give a formal (i.e., mathematically exact) characterization of recursive function.
sentences.zh: 罗杰斯“递归函数理论和有效可计算性”，第 1 页，重点是作者的：§ 1.1 算法的非正式概念在本章中，我们给出了递归函数的正式（即数学上精确的）表征。

sentences.en: The concept is basic for the remainder of the book.
sentences.zh: 这个概念是本书其余部分的基础。

sentences.en: It is one way of making precise the informal mathematical notion of function computable "by algorithm" or "by effective procedure".
sentences.zh: 它是使“通过算法”或“通过有效过程”可计算的函数的非正式数学概念变得精确的一种方法。

sentences.en: In this section, as a preliminary to the formal characterization, we discuss certain aspects of the informal notions of algorithm and function computable by algorithm as they occur in mathematics.I don't understand why you people act like you're stumped by literally, literally page 1 of computer science.
sentences.zh: 在本节中，作为形式表征的初步部分，我们讨论数学中出现的算法和可通过算法计算的函数的非正式概念的某些方面。我不明白为什么你们表现得好像被计算机科学的字面上的第一页难住了。

blocks.html_en:
> I don't understand why you people act like you're stumped by literally, literally page 1 of computer science.

… and yet you didn't stop for a moment to consider that in a field as fast-moving as computer science, a concept which might not have had a formal definition in 1967 acquired one in the past 59 years? See, for example, Sipser's Introduction to the Theory of Computation, which has an entire section (3.3 in my 1997 print) titled “The Definiton of Algorithm”.

blocks.html_en: Yes, did you read that section? It's using different terminology but it's saying exactly what I'm saying (of course it does, it's elementary computer science). There is no accepted definition of the word "algorithm": there wasn't one in 1967, there wasn't one in 1997 and there isn't one in 2026. There are models of computations we can prove to be equivalent to each other, but no commonly accepted definition that captures the way we use the word in normal speech.

blocks.html_en: If you pair LLMs/AIs like Claude with a formal verification framework you essentially have a new sort of compiler.

sentences.en: If you pair LLMs/AIs like Claude with a formal verification framework you essentially have a new sort of compiler.
sentences.zh: 如果你将像克劳德这样的法学硕士/人工智能与正式的验证框架结合起来，你基本上就拥有了一种新型编译器。

blocks.html_en: If you pair LLMs/AIs like Claude with a formal verification framework you essentially have a new sort of compiler.

sentences.en: If you pair LLMs/AIs like Claude with a formal verification framework you essentially have a new sort of compiler.

blocks.html_en: That’s technically correct, but it still means that someone has to write the formal specs that Claude’s output is verified against, and someone has to design the formal languages the specs are written in. And once you have formal specs that truly covers all aspects that you ever will care about, it’s unclear if you couldn’t instead build a non-LLM mechanism that efficiently and deterministically spits out an implementation of such a spec.

sentences.en: That’s technically correct, but it still means that someone has to write the formal specs that Claude’s output is verified against, and someone has to design the formal languages the specs are written in.
sentences.zh: 这在技术上是正确的，但这仍然意味着必须有人编写正式规范来验证 Claude 的输出，并且必须有人设计编写规范所用的正式语言。

sentences.en: And once you have formal specs that truly covers all aspects that you ever will care about, it’s unclear if you couldn’t instead build a non-LLM mechanism that efficiently and deterministically spits out an implementation of such a spec.
sentences.zh: 一旦你有了真正涵盖你所关心的所有方面的正式规范，就不清楚你是否不能构建一个非 LLM 机制来高效、确定地提出此类规范的实现。

blocks.html_en:
I expect in the future it will become hybrid. Formal specs provide guardrails for LLMs / AI but within those guards LLMs will author traditional compiler programs and at times drop-down to manually compile / prove certain snippets.

Could we describe the first humans manually writing Assembly and their own higher level abstractions as "compilers"? Loosely I think we could, and LLMs fit the same mold.

sentences.en: I expect in the future it will become hybrid.
sentences.zh: 我预计未来它将成为混合动力。

sentences.en: Formal specs provide guardrails for LLMs / AI but within those guards LLMs will author traditional compiler programs and at times drop-down to manually compile / prove certain snippets.Could we describe the first humans manually writing Assembly and their own higher level abstractions as "compilers"?
sentences.zh: 正式规范为法学硕士/人工智能提供了护栏，但在这些护栏内，法学硕士将编写传统的编译器程序，有时会下拉手动编译/证明某些片段。我们可以将第一批手动编写汇编和他们自己的更高级别抽象的人描述为“编译器”吗？

sentences.en: Loosely I think we could, and LLMs fit the same mold.
sentences.zh: 大致上我认为我们可以，法学硕士也符合同样的模式。

blocks.html_en:
I disagree in the sense that source code (compiler input) leaves little space as to the behavior of the resulting program. With LLMs, either there is much more implied leeway, in which case I’d argue that it’s different in nature. Or the specs will restrict the LLM output to a similar extent as a conventional programming language restricts a compiler’s freedoms, in which case it’s not clear that LLMs are the most expedient technology for generating that output.

Levels of abstraction actually matter with regard to how much observable behavior they leave unspecified, and with regard to how much control is needed over each respective behavioral aspect.

sentences.en: I disagree in the sense that source code (compiler input) leaves little space as to the behavior of the resulting program.
sentences.zh: 我不同意源代码（编译器输入）对于结果程序的行为留下的空间很小。

sentences.en: With LLMs, either there is much more implied leeway, in which case I’d argue that it’s different in nature.
sentences.zh: 对于法学硕士，要么有更多隐含的回旋余地，在这种情况下，我认为它本质上是不同的。

sentences.en: Or the specs will restrict the LLM output to a similar extent as a conventional programming language restricts a compiler’s freedoms, in which case it’s not clear that LLMs are the most expedient technology for generating that output.Levels of abstraction actually matter with regard to how much observable behavior they leave unspecified, and with regard to how much control is needed over each respective behavioral aspect.
sentences.zh: 或者，规范将限制 LLM 输出到类似于传统编程语言限制编译器自由的程度，在这种情况下，尚不清楚 LLM 是否是生成该输出的最便捷技术。抽象级别实际上很重要，因为它们留下了多少未指定的可观察行为，以及需要对每个各自的行为方面进行多少控制。

blocks.html_en:
And claude isnt a replacement for the chain of humans, you dont know what you dont know, thats why we have a person in a role to gatekeep each step.

Anyone using claude can see it misses steps that an engineer wouldnt, it can make bad choices that a sysadmin wouldnt, it can pick the wrong order a project manager wouldnt, it can pick wrong cost models that a bean counter wouldnt, etc etc etc

But, I'm pretending it is and living with those mistakes.

sentences.en: And claude isnt a replacement for the chain of humans, you dont know what you dont know, thats why we have a person in a role to gatekeep each step.Anyone using claude can see it misses steps that an engineer wouldnt, it can make bad choices that a sysadmin wouldnt, it can pick the wrong order a project manager wouldnt, it can pick wrong cost models that a bean counter wouldnt, etc etc etcBut, I'm pretending it is and living with those mistakes.
sentences.zh: 克劳德并不是人类链条的替代品，你不知道你不知道什么，这就是为什么我们有一个人来把关每一步。任何使用克劳德的人都可以看到它错过了工程师不会的步骤，它可以做出系统管理员不会做出的错误选择，它可以选择项目经理不会的错误订单，它可以选择豆计数器不会的错误成本模型，等等等等但是，我假装它是这样的，并生活在这些错误中。

blocks.html_en:
Claude is more like a Probabilistic Turing Machine. [0] It's correctness isn't deterministic but rather a distribution. It is predictable.

There are lots of places in computer science that determinism isn't necessarily the best.

- UDP video calls let packets vanish or arrive corrupted, because waiting for retransmissions would freeze the picture.

- Stochastic gradient descent picks a random mini-batch of training data and treats it as the whole dataset, because computing the true gradient on every step would make training infeasible.

- Speculative execution in modern CPUs guesses which branch a program will take and rolls back when wrong, because waiting for the real answer would leave half the silicon idle.

What we want is something like a Las Vegas algorithm, a probabilistic machine with a cheap verifier.

[0] https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Probabilistic_Turing_machine

sentences.en: Claude is more like a Probabilistic Turing Machine. [0] It's correctness isn't deterministic but rather a distribution.
sentences.zh: 克劳德更像是一个概率图灵机。 [0] 它的正确性不是确定性的，而是一种分布。

sentences.en: It is predictable.There are lots of places in computer science that determinism isn't necessarily the best.- UDP video calls let packets vanish or arrive corrupted, because waiting for retransmissions would freeze the picture.- Stochastic gradient descent picks a random mini-batch of training data and treats it as the whole dataset, because computing the true gradient on every step would make training infeasible.- Speculative execution in modern CPUs guesses which branch a program will take and rolls back when wrong, because waiting for the real answer would leave half the silicon idle.What we want is something like a Las Vegas algorithm, a probabilistic machine with a cheap verifier.[0] https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Probabilistic_Turing_machine
sentences.zh: 这是可以预测的。在计算机科学中有很多地方，确定性不一定是最好的。- UDP 视频通话会让数据包消失或损坏，因为等待重传会冻结图像。- 随机梯度下降随机选择一小批训练数据并将其视为整个数据集，因为计算每一步的真实梯度会使训练变得不可行。- 现代 CPU 中的推测执行会猜测程序将采用哪个分支，并在错误时回滚，因为等待因为真正的答案会让一半的芯片闲置。我们想要的是拉斯维加斯算法，一个带有廉价验证器的概率机器。[0] https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Probabilistic_Turing_machine

sentences.en: It is predictable.There are lots of places in computer science that determinism isn't necessarily the best.- UDP video calls let packets vanish or arrive corrupted, because waiting for retransmissions would freeze the picture.- Stochastic gradient descent picks a random mini-batch of training data and treats it as the whole dataset, because computing the true gradient on every step would make training infeasible.- Speculative execution in modern CPUs guesses which branch a program will take and rolls back when wrong, because waiting for the real answer would leave half the silicon idle.What we want is something like a Las Vegas algorithm, a probabilistic machine with a cheap verifier.[0] https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Probabilistic_Turing_machine
sentences.zh: 这是可以预测的。在计算机科学中有很多地方，确定性不一定是最好的。- UDP 视频通话会让数据包消失或损坏，因为等待重传会冻结图像。- 随机梯度下降随机选择一小批训练数据并将其视为整个数据集，因为计算每一步的真实梯度会使训练变得不可行。- 现代 CPU 中的推测执行会猜测程序将采用哪个分支，并在错误时回滚，因为等待因为真正的答案会让一半的芯片闲置。我们想要的是拉斯维加斯算法，一个带有廉价验证器的概率机器。[0] https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Probabilistic_Turing_machine

blocks.html_en: Claude is more like a Probabilistic Turing Machine. [0] It's correctness isn't deterministic but rather a distribution. It is predictable.

sentences.en: Claude is more like a Probabilistic Turing Machine. [0] It's correctness isn't deterministic but rather a distribution.
sentences.zh: 克劳德更像是一个概率图灵机。 [0] 它的正确性不是确定性的，而是一种分布。

sentences.en: It is predictable.
sentences.zh: 这是可以预见的。

blocks.html_en: There are lots of places in computer science that determinism isn't necessarily the best.

sentences.en: There are lots of places in computer science that determinism isn't necessarily the best.
sentences.zh: 在计算机科学中有很多地方，决定论不一定是最好的。

blocks.html_en: - UDP video calls let packets vanish or arrive corrupted, because waiting for retransmissions would freeze the picture.

sentences.en: - UDP video calls let packets vanish or arrive corrupted, because waiting for retransmissions would freeze the picture.
sentences.zh: - UDP 视频通话会让数据包消失或到达时损坏，因为等待重传会冻结图像。

blocks.html_en: - Stochastic gradient descent picks a random mini-batch of training data and treats it as the whole dataset, because computing the true gradient on every step would make training infeasible.

sentences.en: - Stochastic gradient descent picks a random mini-batch of training data and treats it as the whole dataset, because computing the true gradient on every step would make training infeasible.
sentences.zh: - 随机梯度下降选择随机的小批量训练数据并将其视为整个数据集，因为在每一步计算真实梯度将使训练变得不可行。

blocks.html_en: - Speculative execution in modern CPUs guesses which branch a program will take and rolls back when wrong, because waiting for the real answer would leave half the silicon idle.

sentences.en: - Speculative execution in modern CPUs guesses which branch a program will take and rolls back when wrong, because waiting for the real answer would leave half the silicon idle.
sentences.zh: - 现代 CPU 中的推测执行会猜测程序将采用哪个分支，并在错误时回滚，因为等待真正的答案会使一半的芯片闲置。

blocks.html_en: What we want is something like a Las Vegas algorithm, a probabilistic machine with a cheap verifier.

sentences.en: What we want is something like a Las Vegas algorithm, a probabilistic machine with a cheap verifier.
sentences.zh: 我们想要的是类似拉斯维加斯算法的东西，一种带有廉价验证器的概率机器。

blocks.html_en:
Determinism isn’t important, but there are usually threshold of acceptable performance in all off those cases. Machine Learning was all about improving accuracy even when you know that there’s probably an error somewhere. But the current LLM hypers refuses to acknowledge that the answer to a prompt may be wrong (even though the providers have a warning line about it).

Instead of assessing the risk and provide corrective methods, all they do is pushing to use it more and more everywhere.

sentences.en: Determinism isn’t important, but there are usually threshold of acceptable performance in all off those cases.
sentences.zh: 确定性并不重要，但在所有这些情况下通常都有可接受的性能阈值。

sentences.en: Machine Learning was all about improving accuracy even when you know that there’s probably an error somewhere.
sentences.zh: 机器学习的目的就是提高准确性，即使您知道某个地方可能存在错误。

sentences.en: But the current LLM hypers refuses to acknowledge that the answer to a prompt may be wrong (even though the providers have a warning line about it).Instead of assessing the risk and provide corrective methods, all they do is pushing to use it more and more everywhere.
sentences.zh: 但目前的法学硕士超级学生拒绝承认提示的答案可能是错误的（即使提供者对此有警告线）。他们所做的不是评估风险并提供纠正方法，而是推动在各地越来越多地使用它。

blocks.html_en:
> I’d say that, in all the ways that matter, I understand the code. Sure, if I had to hand-edit it now, there’d be a serious learning curve. But I won't have to.

Famous last words, but point is taken.

The non deterministic nature of an llm breaks the metaphor that they are like a compiler.

However, it’s not foreign to compilers to receive feedback from the running program (PGOs), so there are still parallels to the feedback we provide LLMs that guide their “optimization”.

I think even calling LLMs a non-deterministic compiler isn’t accurate either so ultimately I agree the metaphor doesn’t quite work.

I do think LLMs are like compilers in terms of how they changed how we build programs from a historical context. But that’s about it.

sentences.en: > I’d say that, in all the ways that matter, I understand the code.
sentences.zh: > 我想说的是，在所有重要的方面，我理解代码。

sentences.en: Sure, if I had to hand-edit it now, there’d be a serious learning curve.
sentences.zh: 当然，如果我现在必须手动编辑它，就会有一个严重的学习曲线。

sentences.en: But I won't have to.Famous last words, but point is taken.The non deterministic nature of an llm breaks the metaphor that they are like a compiler.However, it’s not foreign to compilers to receive feedback from the running program (PGOs), so there are still parallels to the feedback we provide LLMs that guide their “optimization”.I think even calling LLMs a non-deterministic compiler isn’t accurate either so ultimately I agree the metaphor doesn’t quite work.I do think LLMs are like compilers in terms of how they changed how we build programs from a historical context.
sentences.zh: 但我不必这样做。著名的最后一句话，但要点是。LLM 的非确定性本质打破了它们就像编译器的比喻。然而，编译器从正在运行的程序（PGO）接收反馈并不陌生，因此与我们提供的指导其“优化”的 LLM 的反馈仍然有相似之处。我认为即使将 LLM 称为非确定性编译器也不准确，所以最终我同意这个比喻并不完全正确。我确实认为法学硕士就像编译器，因为它们改变了我们从历史背景构建程序的方式。

sentences.en: But that’s about it.
sentences.zh: 但仅此而已。

blocks.html_en: The non-determinism is theoretical solvable. Chaos (prompt instability) isn’t. When you change a single word in the input, you get a completely different output. Because of this LLMs will never be compilers.

sentences.en: The non-determinism is theoretical solvable.
sentences.zh: 非决定论在理论上是可以解决的。

sentences.en: Chaos (prompt instability) isn’t.
sentences.zh: 混乱（迅速不稳定）则不然。

sentences.en: When you change a single word in the input, you get a completely different output.
sentences.zh: 当您更改输入中的单个单词时，您会得到完全不同的输出。

sentences.en: Because of this LLMs will never be compilers.
sentences.zh: 因此，法学硕士永远不会成为编译器。

blocks.html_en:
Because Claude isn't a human the perception of it is different.

Humans can fly planes, computers too can fly planes. I'm not going to give the same leeway to a 'computer' flying a plane that I would to a human. Because we understand that humans are just that, humans. The demands placed on a computer are so very different.

sentences.en: Because Claude isn't a human the perception of it is different.Humans can fly planes, computers too can fly planes.
sentences.zh: 因为克劳德不是人类，所以对它的感知是不同的。人类可以驾驶飞机，计算机也可以驾驶飞机。

sentences.en: I'm not going to give the same leeway to a 'computer' flying a plane that I would to a human.
sentences.zh: 我不会给驾驶飞机的“计算机”提供与人类相同的余地。

sentences.en: Because we understand that humans are just that, humans.
sentences.zh: 因为我们知道人类就是人类。

sentences.en: The demands placed on a computer are so very different.
sentences.zh: 对计算机的要求非常不同。

blocks.html_en:
Because Claude isn't a human the perception of it is different.

Humans can fly planes, computers too can fly planes. I'm not going to give the same leeway to a 'computer' flying a plane that I would to a human. Because we understand that humans are just that, humans. The demands placed on a computer are so very different.

blocks.html_en:
What about driving a car?

Computers are already driving cars autonomously, possibly better than the average human already, and quite plausibly will eventually be better than the best human driver.

sentences.en: What about driving a car?Computers are already driving cars autonomously, possibly better than the average human already, and quite plausibly will eventually be better than the best human driver.
sentences.zh: 驾驶汽车怎么样？计算机已经可以自动驾驶汽车，可能已经比普通人类驾驶得更好，而且很有可能最终会比最好的人类驾驶员更好。

blocks.html_en: Precisely my point. Say for examples planes, trains or automobiles, humans may make way more errors than a computer does but you can bet your bottom dollar the threshold for allowing computers to autonomously control the above is way higher. Because the expectation is that if it is a computer it ought do it far better/safer.

sentences.en: Precisely my point.
sentences.zh: 这正是我的观点。

sentences.en: Say for examples planes, trains or automobiles, humans may make way more errors than a computer does but you can bet your bottom dollar the threshold for allowing computers to autonomously control the above is way higher.
sentences.zh: 比如说飞机、火车或汽车，人类可能会比计算机犯更多的错误，但你可以打赌，允许计算机自主控制上述的门槛要高得多。

sentences.en: Because the expectation is that if it is a computer it ought do it far better/safer.
sentences.zh: 因为人们的期望是，如果它是一台计算机，它应该做得更好/更安全。

blocks.html_en: There is a lot of rehashing of LLM arguments in the comments here, but I'd love to read an actual distributed systems expert's take on the DNS architecture the author and/or Claude ended up with. One of my personal fears about LLM coding is that some of those implicitly outsourced decisions turn out to matter more than expected. Does the "timeline" stamp make sense? (I know there's not much detail to work from.)

blocks.html_en:
> exe.dev VMs have nice domain names: vm-name.exe.xyz. When we start a new VM, we add a CNAME entry or three. Easy, right?

> But our VMs start fast, so fast that even if we created the DNS entries before creating the VM, our users still had to sit around waiting for DNS to propagate, which occasionally took minutes, not seconds.

> We did the obvious thing: We wrote our own DNS server, so that DNS always immediately matched the source of truth. And life was good.

> But latency matters, so we added regions. And just like that, DNS became the long pole again, because all DNS was served out of Oregon. Also, deployments caused tiny DNS outages. To fix this, all we needed now was a geographically distributed but fully consistent DNS server.

> We did what a sensible engineer does when faced with a hard problem: cheat. We vibe-engineered a distributed DNS server tuned to our specific needs.

I'm sure they have already considered this, but what problem does this solve that couldn't be solved by using dnsmasq, unbound or something like that? Why reinvent the wheel?

sentences.en: > exe.dev VMs have nice domain names: vm-name.exe.xyz.
sentences.zh: > exe.dev VM 有很好的域名：vm-name.exe.xyz。

sentences.en: When we start a new VM, we add a CNAME entry or three.
sentences.zh: 当我们启动新的虚拟机时，我们会添加一个或三个 CNAME 条目。

sentences.en: Easy, right?> But our VMs start fast, so fast that even if we created the DNS entries before creating the VM, our users still had to sit around waiting for DNS to propagate, which occasionally took minutes, not seconds.> We did the obvious thing: We wrote our own DNS server, so that DNS always immediately matched the source of truth.
sentences.zh: 很简单，对吧？> 但是我们的虚拟机启动速度很快，快到即使我们在创建虚拟机之前创建了 DNS 条目，我们的用户仍然必须等待 DNS 传播，这有时需要几分钟，而不是几秒钟。> 我们做了一件显而易见的事情：我们编写了自己的 DNS 服务器，以便 DNS 始终立即匹配事实来源。

sentences.en: And life was good.> But latency matters, so we added regions.
sentences.zh: 生活很美好。> 但延迟很重要，所以我们添加了区域。

sentences.en: And just like that, DNS became the long pole again, because all DNS was served out of Oregon.
sentences.zh: 就这样，DNS 再次成为长杆，因为所有 DNS 均由俄勒冈州提供服务。

sentences.en: Also, deployments caused tiny DNS outages.
sentences.zh: 此外，部署还导致了轻微的 DNS 中断。

sentences.en: To fix this, all we needed now was a geographically distributed but fully consistent DNS server.> We did what a sensible engineer does when faced with a hard problem: cheat.
sentences.zh: 为了解决这个问题，我们现在需要的是一个地理上分散但完全一致的 DNS 服务器。> 我们做了一个明智的工程师在面临难题时会做的事情：作弊。

sentences.en: We vibe-engineered a distributed DNS server tuned to our specific needs.I'm sure they have already considered this, but what problem does this solve that couldn't be solved by using dnsmasq, unbound or something like that?
sentences.zh: 我们根据我们的特定需求设计了一个分布式 DNS 服务器。我相信他们已经考虑过这一点，但是这解决了什么问题是使用 dnsmasq、unbound 或类似的东西无法解决的呢？

sentences.en: Why reinvent the wheel?
sentences.zh: 为什么要重新发明轮子？

blocks.html_en: The question is more what this really solves. They said they built all this because DNS propagation was too slow. But secondary dns servers by e.g. your isp will still have old records when you change something. So what did they earn? Every is correct within their network pretty fast. But for a normal user there isnt any difference because 8.8.8.8 or 1.1.1.1 still cache old records?

sentences.en: The question is more what this really solves.
sentences.zh: 问题更多的是这真正解决了什么。

sentences.en: They said they built all this because DNS propagation was too slow.
sentences.zh: 他们说他们构建这一切是因为 DNS 传播太慢了。

sentences.en: But secondary dns servers by e.g. your isp will still have old records when you change something.
sentences.zh: 但辅助 DNS 服务器例如当您更改某些内容时，您的 ISP 仍将保留旧记录。

sentences.en: So what did they earn?
sentences.zh: 那么他们赚了什么？

sentences.en: Every is correct within their network pretty fast.
sentences.zh: 他们的网络中的每一个都非常快地正确。

sentences.en: But for a normal user there isnt any difference because 8.8.8.8 or 1.1.1.1 still cache old records?
sentences.zh: 但对于普通用户来说没有任何区别，因为 8.8.8.8 或 1.1.1.1 仍然缓存旧记录？

blocks.html_en:
This seems to be grasping for analogies to make sense of the work this person was doing.

A compiler does a lot more than source code translation.

There are specifications that tell us the de jure specifications of the language, if there is one, and then we have to recognize the de facto implementations of said language. The often disagree and leave much on the table. Some times on purpose, such as implementation details, and other times by omission.

Users of this compiler expect a deterministic compilation of the source text into the target code but it’s rarely 1:1. There are optimization passes, inlining, barriers, etc.

And then there are the run-time effects of executing a program!

I think the analogy gets a little weak because natural language is not a precise enough language to specify discrete systems.

What it sounds like the author is doing is bypassing decision points with other people and delaying making decisions themselves until the LLM agent forces them to? Which is a fine approach but I don’t think the analogy with a compiler is necessary.

People seem to have a hard enough time understanding branch prediction and thread barriers.

sentences.en: This seems to be grasping for analogies to make sense of the work this person was doing.A compiler does a lot more than source code translation.There are specifications that tell us the de jure specifications of the language, if there is one, and then we have to recognize the de facto implementations of said language.
sentences.zh: 这似乎是在抓住类比来理解这个人正在做的工作。编译器所做的不仅仅是源代码翻译。有一些规范告诉我们该语言的法律规范（如果有的话），然后我们必须认识到所述语言的事实上的实现。

sentences.en: The often disagree and leave much on the table.
sentences.zh: 他们常常意见不一，并留下很多问题。

sentences.en: Some times on purpose, such as implementation details, and other times by omission.Users of this compiler expect a deterministic compilation of the source text into the target code but it’s rarely 1:1.
sentences.zh: 有时是故意的，例如实现细节，有时是遗漏的。该编译器的用户期望将源文本确定性地编译为目标代码，但很少是 1:1。

sentences.en: There are optimization passes, inlining, barriers, etc.And then there are the run-time effects of executing a program!I think the analogy gets a little weak because natural language is not a precise enough language to specify discrete systems.What it sounds like the author is doing is bypassing decision points with other people and delaying making decisions themselves until the LLM agent forces them to?
sentences.zh: 有优化过程、内联、障碍等。然后还有执行程序的运行时效果！我认为这个类比有点弱，因为自然语言不是一种足够精确的语言来指定离散系统。听起来作者正在做的是绕过其他人的决策点并延迟自己做出决策，直到 LLM 代理强迫他们这样做？

sentences.en: Which is a fine approach but I don’t think the analogy with a compiler is necessary.People seem to have a hard enough time understanding branch prediction and thread barriers.
sentences.zh: 这是一个很好的方法，但我认为与编译器的类比是没有必要的。人们似乎很难理解分支预测和线程障碍。

blocks.html_en:
I still think it is (in the source-code generating mode) a compiler, just like LiquidHaskell is. (I haven't used LH but it essentially can automatically supply functions based on the type conditions you specify.)

In my view, reasoning LLMs have learned a large number of close to true sentences of "logic" in natural language. These rules are not consistent (unlike typing rules of LH that allow creation of provably correct programs), but they work in practice better than LH. Essentially, they represent an unsound formal specification of natural language, together with many useful (almost)tautologies that helps solving constraint problems (just like LH does, but again unlike LLM, provably correctly).

LLMs proved that human language can be formalized reasonably close to soundness. I believe we can have a sound formalization of human language, and that kind of formal language will be once superior to LLMs.

It will also not be as complicated as LLMs and require only fraction of compute to use.

sentences.en: I still think it is (in the source-code generating mode) a compiler, just like LiquidHaskell is.
sentences.zh: 我仍然认为它（在源代码生成模式下）是一个编译器，就像 LiquidHaskell 一样。

sentences.en: (I haven't used LH but it essentially can automatically supply functions based on the type conditions you specify.)In my view, reasoning LLMs have learned a large number of close to true sentences of "logic" in natural language.
sentences.zh: （我没有使用过LH，但它本质上可以根据你指定的类型条件自动提供函数。）在我看来，推理LLM已经在自然语言中学习了大量接近真实“逻辑”的句子。

sentences.en: These rules are not consistent (unlike typing rules of LH that allow creation of provably correct programs), but they work in practice better than LH.
sentences.zh: 这些规则并不一致（与允许创建可证明正确的程序的 LH 键入规则不同），但它们在实践中比 LH 更有效。

sentences.en: Essentially, they represent an unsound formal specification of natural language, together with many useful (almost)tautologies that helps solving constraint problems (just like LH does, but again unlike LLM, provably correctly).LLMs proved that human language can be formalized reasonably close to soundness.
sentences.zh: 本质上，它们代表了自然语言的不健全的形式规范，以及许多有用的（几乎）同义反复，有助于解决约束问题（就像 LH 所做的那样，但又不同于法学硕士，可证明是正确的）。法学硕士证明人类语言可以被形式化得相当接近健全性。

sentences.en: I believe we can have a sound formalization of human language, and that kind of formal language will be once superior to LLMs.It will also not be as complicated as LLMs and require only fraction of compute to use.
sentences.zh: 我相信我们可以对人类语言进行完善的形式化，这种形式化语言将一度优于LLM。它也不会像LLM那么复杂，只需要一小部分计算即可使用。

blocks.html_en: The entire "Don't Read the Code" argument is just insane to me and ironically when you see who the people are that are advocating it, it's often the same people that talked about microservices, the latest java script framework, no-code or any other methodology that was in vogue at the time just to avoid focusing on solving the real problems; The illusion that they are just one "smart step" away from achieving a 10x productivity boost that will make everything else trivial...

sentences.en: The entire "Don't Read the Code" argument is just insane to me and ironically when you see who the people are that are advocating it, it's often the same people that talked about microservices, the latest java script framework, no-code or any other methodology that was in vogue at the time just to avoid focusing on solving the real problems; The illusion that they are just one "smart step" away from achieving a 10x productivity boost that will make everything else trivial...
sentences.zh: 整个“不要阅读代码”的论点对我来说简直是疯狂，讽刺的是，当你看到倡导它的人是谁时，往往是同一个人在谈论微服务、最新的 java 脚本框架、无代码或当时流行的任何其他方法，只是为了避免专注于解决真正的问题；一种错觉，认为他们距离实现 10 倍生产力提升仅“明智的一步”，而这将使其他一切变得微不足道......

blocks.html_en:
> By the time I was ready to build a keeper, I had accumulated a scar-tissue document that was empirically sufficient to guide an agent through most of the important decisions, at every layer, ranging from high level goals through architecture down to the occasional low level detail, such as the exact shape of the data type for load-bearing concurrent caches.

Waterfall is dead, long live waterfall!

sentences.en: > By the time I was ready to build a keeper, I had accumulated a scar-tissue document that was empirically sufficient to guide an agent through most of the important decisions, at every layer, ranging from high level goals through architecture down to the occasional low level detail, such as the exact shape of the data type for load-bearing concurrent caches.Waterfall is dead, long live waterfall!
sentences.zh: > 当我准备好构建一个 keeper 时，我已经积累了一份伤痕累累的文档，根据经验，该文档足以指导代理在每一层完成大多数重要决策，从高层目标到架构，再到偶尔的低层细节，例如承载并发缓存的数据类型的确切形状。瀑布已死，瀑布万岁！

blocks.html_en:
I'll go a step further and say Claude is an Interpreter.

It even has just-in-time compilation: it can directly follow a spec and generate small snippets live.

I'm pretty sure we'll soon see services (admittedly highly inefficient) built on Claude-as-a-backend.

blocks.html_en:
Claude doesn't even make machine code though. Or it can if you really want to, but that's now how people use it. If you wanna include transpilers or interpreters, fine but the answer is still no, otherwise our git commits would just be prompts that get built either in CI or JIT. People deliver code, not prompts.

Maybe that could change some day, but even then, it wouldn't be a useful designation. It's like saying "oh technically the Americas are an island." There's a reason we still distinguish between a transpiler and compiler.

blocks.html_en: I'm kind of getting to the point where if I know something is vibe coded, I just won't use it. It's not an anti-AI thing, it's just a quality thing. Pretty much every piece of vibe coded software I've used has been bad in some regard. The worst ones are the ones that aren't obviously bad but rather subtly bad in dangerous ways (the article about OpenCode yesterday definitely made me nope out of using that)

blocks.html_en:
> I’d say that, in all the ways that matter, I understand the code. Sure, if I had to hand-edit it now, there’d be a serious learning curve. But I won't have to. And more importantly, I can reason about the system, share perspectives with my colleagues, and guide agents on future work.

If there's a serious learning curve to editing code then you don't understand the code. We used to call that 'on-boarding' when you brought a new engineer on the team as they got up to speed as to how the codebase worked.

sentences.en: > I’d say that, in all the ways that matter, I understand the code.
sentences.zh: > 我想说的是，在所有重要的方面，我理解代码。

sentences.en: Sure, if I had to hand-edit it now, there’d be a serious learning curve.
sentences.zh: 当然，如果我现在必须手动编辑它，就会有一个严重的学习曲线。

sentences.en: But I won't have to.
sentences.zh: 但我不必这么做。

sentences.en: And more importantly, I can reason about the system, share perspectives with my colleagues, and guide agents on future work.If there's a serious learning curve to editing code then you don't understand the code.
sentences.zh: 更重要的是，我可以推理系统，与同事分享观点，并指导代理未来的工作。如果编辑代码有一个严格的学习曲线，那么你就无法理解代码。

sentences.en: We used to call that 'on-boarding' when you brought a new engineer on the team as they got up to speed as to how the codebase worked.
sentences.zh: 当你在团队中引入一名新工程师时，我们过去称之为“入职”，因为他们可以加快了解代码库的工作方式。

> Among other things, he drew an analogy between LLMs and compilers.

blocks.html_en: Spec-driven development is sold on the idea that you regenerate modules or entire services when you change the spec. It's a really dumb idea but was briefly popular in enterprise, probably still is in some companies.

blocks.html_en:
A compiler does not transalte specs to code.

Specs are denotational by nature.

A program could synthesize a program that adheres to the specs. But that is not what we understand by a compiler that generally has to preserve operational semantics.

sentences.en: A compiler does not transalte specs to code.Specs are denotational by nature.A program could synthesize a program that adheres to the specs.
sentences.zh: 编译器不会将规范转换为代码。规范本质上是指称的。程序可以合成符合规范的程序。

sentences.en: But that is not what we understand by a compiler that generally has to preserve operational semantics.
sentences.zh: 但这并不是我们所理解的通常必须保留操作语义的编译器。

content_html_zh: LLM-is-a-compiler 确实是一种简单化的方法。 我对昨天的 Cursor 帖子进行了反驳，可以在这里重用它 https://replicated.live/blog/follow-up 835 页规范“就存在”并且我们运行 LLM 来实现它的想法是完全有缺陷的。 规范并不是凭空出现的，它们与代码共同演化。 如果你有代码，为什么要再次生成它？好的软件是无数反馈循环的产物，法学硕士可以让你更快地操作这些循环。 但它们并不能补充整个过程。最终，好的产品是一桶经过提炼的反馈。我（甚至是法学硕士预科生）一直觉得有趣的事情是——所有可以在计算机上运行的代码都已经存在。 它是所有可用内存位的某种排列。 它就在那儿的某个地方。 因此，假设您想要一个特定的程序。1。 这些可能的程序中有很大一部分显然不是您想要的（大多数甚至无法编译）。2。 其余的程序可能看起来与您想要的程序类似，但有足够多的错误，以至于完全无法使用、无法读取到难以处理的程度，等等。3。 其余程序看起来基本正确，但使用超过几分钟后，您会发现一些重大错误，使其仍然不够正确。4。 其余的程序看起来基本上是正确的，并且似乎通常符合您的意愿，但包含一个长尾的致命错误，它会损坏保存的数据，或者使所有输出稍微不正确，等等5。 其余的程序可能有用，即使它们有点烦人。6。 这个过程可能会持续数千次迭代，直到您最终找到“它”。 你想要的程序。 或者... 其中之一。 现阶段可能还剩下 10,000 多个候选项目。我们的工作一直是达到 5 个并力争达到 6 个。 事实上，大多数软件开发只是在一个循环中进行 5->6。我认为 LLM 可以帮助我们比以前更快地达到 3 到 5 之间的目标。 他们的一个大问题是，3 vs 5 中的程序看起来基本上都是正确的，并且没有办法知道你得到的是 3 还是 5。一般来说，上述并不是法学硕士预科生编写软件的_感觉_，这只是一种想象你正在做什么的可爱方式。 现在却很奇怪。我曾经看过一个 YouTube 视频，其中两个初学者正在学习基础编程，打开 cmd 提示符后，其中一个人说“如果你在这个框中输入正确的单词，你就可以窃取阿肯色州某人的 401K，这太疯狂了”（或者类似的内容）。 从技术上讲，这是相当正确的，这一点一直困扰着我。 这可能不是你会做的，但是使用正确的魔法词以正确的顺序它会起作用我的一位教授用这样的例子来鼓励你在发现自己在做类似“也许如果我把这个 < 翻转到 > 测试就会通过”之类的事情时停下来。 与正确程序的空间相比，错误程序（即使是通过测试的程序）的空间是巨大的。如果您喜欢思考这个问题并且以前没有见过这个问题，请查看繁忙的海狸问题[1]。[1]：https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Busy_beaver“计算机科学中有两个难题：缓存失效、命名事物和偏一错误。”所有可以在计算机上运行的代码都已经存在。 它是所有可用内存位的某种排列。 它就在那儿的某个地方这在数学上似乎是不可能的或空洞的，具体取决于你的意思......我认为意图很明确。 我们可以按照大小和字典顺序枚举所有字符串。 那么任务只是过滤，哪些字符串是我们想要的程序。这与“打字本身不是问题，困难的是按钮按下的顺序”是一样的。当然，根据赖斯定理，所有有趣的问题无论如何都是不可判定的......如果我们将自己限制在可以存储在物理宇宙中的程序，突然间一切都从不可判定变为 O(1) :)这与一些艺术家在考虑用原石制作雕像时的心态相同：那里有一个希腊神，你只需要找到他。这是一种思维方式，而不是一些深刻的形而上学之谜。 除非它实际上是一个深刻的形而上学之谜，但我宁愿不去那里。你应该看看博尔赫斯的《巴别塔图书馆》。基本上，如果你有某种语言的无限字符串，那么你就拥有所有可能的书籍、推文、脚本、潦草的文字和所有曾经写过的东西。 你只需要找到它。那已经是一个广阔的宇宙了。 节目？ 更大的集合。无限长的字符串必须具有比无限长更精致的属性。 重复字符的无限字符串不会编码太多有趣的内容。我相信更重要的是，图书馆包含所有书籍的有限集，这些书籍对语言字符进行（唯一）排列，大小为 N。 在如此大的 N 集合中找到癌症治疗方法的挑战实际上是找到它在哪本书中。> 所有可能在计算机上运行的代码都已经存在你必须小心；这种想法可能会让你陷入 L 空间。 你需要一个非常熟练的图书管理员，可能还需要一袋花生才能出去。我认为这是对我从未用这些术语考虑过的过程的一个很好的抽象，我喜欢它。 它解释了软件开发与其他一些构建过程的不同之处，在其他构建过程中，当您弄清楚如何制作能够产生该结果的东西时，就会发现所需的结果不过，有很多进程具有此属性。 例如：建立家庭、工作空间、厨房、工厂、办公室等以适应特定目的。 当您发现什么在特定空间中效果最好时，您将重构您的工作流程、座位组织、存储系统等以适应您正在做的事情。 它甚至可能有多种有效的配置 - 例如季节性变化，如室外与室内区域。> 这是所有可用内存位的某种排列。 它就在那儿的某个地方。确实如此，但是可能的排列数量太大了，难以理解。 我们将程序限制为 50 MiB，即编译后的二进制文件的大小。 如果我没记错的话，那么内存量可以容纳 2^(2^20 * 50 * 8) 种不同的状态。 也就是 2^419430400 或大约 10^126000000。 作为比较，宇宙的热寂预计将在大约 10^100 年或 10^107 秒内发生。 您需要能够每秒查看超过一百万个程序，才能在宇宙终结之前检查所有程序。我以前也对音乐有过类似的想法。 一切都在白噪音中。 要获得音乐，您只需有选择地删除其中的一部分即可。是的，如果您想编写一个探索程序空间的小游泳者代码，您也会在那里找到！现实是分形的（曼德尔布罗特？） 这就是法学硕士将帮助科学的地方。 它们将帮助我们更快地探索模糊边缘。 法学硕士可能不会找到新的“叶子”，但他们应该能够涵盖许多有用但“无聊”的模糊问题。根据我的经验，规范通常是公开的（或者公众可以花几百美元从标准委员会购买，这在商业方面足够接近），但竞争对手的实现的源代码却无法以任何价格获得。 在许多行业中，成为竞争对手的第一个进入障碍是实施 835 页面（请注意，这些都是新秀号码）规范，构建与现有数据格式或法律框架、网络协议或硬件或其他内容兼容的软件。这是开发人员最终陷入的许多隐形孤岛的另一个例子：1。 有些人正在开发供自己雇主使用的企业软件，该规范的存在只是因为它有助于帮助开发团队管理项目。 在这种情况下，期望规范与代码共同发展是完全合理的。 如果发布被推迟到第三季度，或者由于不可预见的实施困难而必须调整目标，那就这样吧。2。 有些正在为其客户开发合同软件。 该规范作为一种通信工具和要求而存在，允许开发人员知道要构建什么，并通知客户他们所构建的内容已达到计费过程的某个阶段（包括完成）。 你必须遵守时间表，如果你迟到或者没有按照你说的去做，那么你的客户就不会付钱给你。3。 有些正在为抽象客户（B2B 或 B2C）开发软件。 通常，这里的规范是监管或行业标准，实施规范是业务的职能。 您发布更新和新产品是为了制定年终销售目标或狙击竞争对手的公告，而广告与各种标准的兼容性是销售演示中的一个关键要点。错误报告、功能请求，尤其是截止日期在每种情况下都有不同的含义。 人工智能开发代理在每个孤岛中的使用方式也有很大差异！作为编译器角度的反驳：Cursor 所做的基本上是将项目转换为另一种语言，这是编译的一种形式。如果您将反馈提炼成全面涵盖功能需求的规范（可能是文档和测试的组合），那么您可以将代码视为缓存的具体化。 再次生成代码的价值在于针对不同的非功能性需求，例如在不同环境中运行的能力或满足不同的性能特征。我同意迭代，但这是一个关于将散文转化为代码的有效性的正交论证。 您可能会问，LLM 能够翻译 835 页的规范，但规范从何而来？ 遵循构建的软件产品的流程：二进制 < IR < 代码 < 规范 < 提示（又名目标） 对于法学硕士来说，要忠实地履行将规范/提示转换为代码的角色，需要有关问题/动机及其周围约束的足够信息。 通常，这是通过研究或导入经过验证的模块来完成的。 留在这个编译链中的人类角色是对构建工件上游动机的足够精确的表达。编辑例如，SWEbench Pro 应该是衡量法学硕士将散文编译成代码的能力的衡量标准。 这些任务中至少有 15% 是由于规范中含糊不清的文字而未能充分确定的。 对于足够具体的问题，Claude 和 Codex 确实通过自行编写的测试编译为功能代码。 来源：https://github.com/kimjune01/swebench-pro-audit事实上，对于如何利用法学硕士的当前能力，这是一个更加现实和有用的框架。 它们的工作速度如此之快，水平如此之高，以至于您可以加速共同进化过程，并最终得到有效的规范和代码。 您实际上可以使用第二系统（第四？ 第五？ 我忘记了）综合症对你有利，因为扔掉东西太便宜了。 他们不只是编写系统，他们还编写测试工具、基准测试、故障模拟、统计数据……所有这些看起来像是“开销”但让你用数据驱动进化的东西。去年我有点进入这种模式，但没有退后一步对其做出如此连贯的解释。源代码始终是蒸馏的结果，或者至少是结果的关键部分。 我不相信自然语言规范可以取代这个角色。 甚至早在法学硕士之前，在编写规范时，我就经常采用某种形式的形式语言来精确确定规范的某些部分。
